title: Healing of topological defects while crystallizing nanocrystals
summary: Understanding the role of confinement while crystallizing nanocrystals is very relevant for predicting their structure and physical properties. With this aim we perform Langevin dynamics simulations of nanocrystals of the model system of few hundred vortices nucleated in micron-sized superconductors. We study the crystallization dynamics and the low-temperature structural properties of vortex nanocrystals nucleated in field-cooling conditions when changing vortex density or elasticity of the system and physical size of the samples. The low-temperature snapshots obtained in simulations present a healing effect at the edges that is in quantitative agreement with experimental data in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ micron-sized samples. We show that the low-temperature radial distribution of topological defects is a stationary profile frozen at a temperature below the melting line tuned by intrinsic properties of the vortex structure and on the confinement effect. These findings on the dynamics and spatial profile of topological defects can be applied to describe the physical properties of confined soft condensed matter nanocrystals in general.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the role of confinement in crystallizing nanocrystals using Langevin dynamics simulations of vortex nanocrystals in microns-sized superconductors. Results show edge healing effects matching experimental data, and reveal that the radial distribution of topological defects becomes a stationary profile frozen below the melting line. These insights can be applied to understand confined soft condensed matter nanocrystals in general.
AI.motivation: Understanding how confinement affects the crystallization process and structural properties of nanocrystals, which is crucial for predicting their physical properties and behavior in applied contexts.
AI.method: Langevin dynamics simulations of nanocrystals consisting of few hundred vortices in micron-sized superconductors, studying crystallization dynamics and low-temperature structural properties under field-cooling conditions by varying vortex density, system elasticity, and sample physical size.
AI.result: The simulations reveal a healing effect at the edges that quantitatively agrees with experimental data from Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ micron-sized samples. The low-temperature radial distribution of topological defects forms a stationary profile frozen below the melting line, influenced by intrinsic properties of the vortex structure and confinement effects.
AI.conclusion: These findings demonstrate that confinement plays a key role in determining the structural properties of vortex nanocrystals. The dynamics and spatial profile of topological defects can be generalized to describe confined soft condensed matter nanocrystals beyond superconductors.

title: Controlled Manipulation of Intermediate State in a Type-I Superconductor
summary: The intermediate state of type-I superconductors presents a classic paradigm of modulated pattern formation, arising from the competition between short-range attractive and long-range repulsive vortex-vortex interactions. However, direct visualization and, more importantly, active control over the topology and dynamics of these flux structures have remained significant challenges, limiting our ability to manipulate them for fundamental studies and potential applications. Here, using low-temperature magnetic force microscopy, we achieve direct imaging and controllable manipulation of the flux structures in a high-purity tantalum single crystal. We systematically track the evolution of flux morphology - from tubes to stripes - during flux penetration and expulsion, revealing a pronounced topological hysteresis originating from the geometric barrier. Furthermore, we demonstrate precise local control by using the magnetic tip to drag and merge individual flux tubes and to reconfigure entire stripe domains. Under global alternating current (AC) excitation, we discover a reversible stripe-grid-stripe transition, a dynamic reorganization driven by current-induced flux penetration and pinning effects. The corresponding phase diagram shows that the threshold current decreases with magnetic field but increases with AC frequency. Our work establishes a pathway for active flux manipulation in type-I superconductors, revealing rich dynamics and paving the way for flux-based superconducting devices.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates direct imaging and active control of flux structures in type-I superconductors using low-temperature magnetic force microscopy, revealing topological hysteresis and reversible stripe-grid transitions under AC excitation.
AI.motivation: The intermediate state of type-I superconductors exhibits modulated pattern formation from competing vortex interactions, but direct visualization and active control over these flux structures have remained challenging, limiting fundamental studies and applications.
AI.method: Low-temperature magnetic force microscopy was used to image and manipulate flux structures in a high-purity tantalum single crystal. The researchers tracked flux morphology evolution during penetration and expulsion, used the magnetic tip to drag and merge individual flux tubes, and applied global AC excitation to study dynamic reorganization.
AI.result: Researchers observed flux morphology evolution from tubes to stripes during flux penetration and expulsion, discovered pronounced topological hysteresis from the geometric barrier, demonstrated precise local control by dragging and merging flux tubes and reconfiguring stripe domains, and found a reversible stripe-grid-stripe transition under AC excitation. The phase diagram shows threshold current decreases with magnetic field but increases with AC frequency.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a pathway for active flux manipulation in type-I superconductors, revealing rich dynamics and paving the way for flux-based superconducting devices.

title: Pairing mechanism and superconductivity in 1313 phase La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$
summary: Recently, the observation of superconductivity (SC) with $T_c$ $\approx$ 3.6 K in the pressurized 1313 La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ has attracted considerable interest. Here, we systematically investigate the electronic properties and superconducting mechanism of 1313 La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ using density functional theory plus dynamical mean-field theory (DFT+DMFT) and random phase approximation (RPA). Our DFT+DMFT calculations reveal that the single-layer (SL) subsystem exhibits nearly insulating behavior, with the $d_{z^2}$ orbital showing Mott physics, while the trilayer (TL) subsystem remains metallic. This indicates that SC primarily resides in the TL subsystem, whose Ni-$e_g$ orbitals are found to be hole-doped relative to bulk La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$. Based on DFT+DMFT-derived low-energy Hamiltonian, RPA-based analysis yields an $s^{\pm}$-wave pairing symmetry within the TL subsystem. Importantly, we identify two key factors that contribute to the significant suppression of $T_c$ in 1313 La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ compared to bulk La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$. First, the hole doping in the TL subsystem, as established by DMFT, leads to a decreased pairing strength, as confirmed by RPA calculations -- a trend resembling that in bulk La$_4$Ni$_3$O$_{10}$. Second, the SL subsystem acts as a bridge connecting adjacent superconducting TL subsystems, thereby forming an S-N-S Josephson junction. The resulting interlayer Josephson coupling governs the phase coherence between TL subsystems and further suppresses the global $T_c$. Combinedly, our findings suggest that the high-$T_c$ phase in the RP La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ family should be attributed to the 2222 La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ rather than the 1313 La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$.

AI.tldr: Study reveals superconductivity in pressurized 1313 La₃Ni₂O₇ primarily originates from the trilayer subsystem, with s±-wave pairing symmetry. The relatively low Tc (3.6 K) compared to bulk La₄Ni₃O₁₀ is due to hole doping effects and interlayer Josephson coupling through insulating single-layer layers.
AI.motivation: Recent observation of superconductivity with Tc ≈ 3.6 K in pressurized 1313 La₃Ni₂O₇ has attracted considerable interest, prompting systematic investigation of electronic properties and superconducting mechanism
AI.method: DFT+DMFT and RPA calculations to investigate electronic properties and superconducting mechanism of pressurized 1313 La₃Ni₂O₇
AI.result: Single-layer subsystem shows nearly insulating behavior with Mott physics in d_z² orbital; trilayer subsystem remains metallic and hosts superconductivity with hole-doping relative to bulk La₄Ni₃O₁₀. s±-wave pairing symmetry identified. Two factors suppress Tc: hole doping reduces pairing strength, and single-layer subsystems form S-N-S Josephson junctions causing interlayer coupling that suppresses global Tc.
AI.conclusion: The high-Tc phase in RP La₃Ni₂O₇ family should be attributed to the 2222 rather than 1313 phase. The 1313 phase has suppressed Tc due to hole-doping in trilayer subsystem reducing pairing strength, and interlayer Josephson coupling between trilayer subsystems mediated by single-layer subsystems.

title: Superconductivity induced by altermagnetic spin fluctuations in high-pressure MnB$_4$
summary: Recent experiments found superconductivity in nonmagnetic MnB$_4$ with a high critical temperature ($T_{c}$) reaching 14 K at 158 GPa. However, ab initio calculations of the electron-phonon coupling predict a $T_{c}$ below 1 K, suggesting that a conventional mechanism cannot explain this phenomenon. In this Letter, we find that MnB$_4$ is close to an altermagnetic instability in density-functional theory calculations. We propose that the superconductivity is driven by altermagnetic spin fluctuations. To verify the pairing symmetry, we have constructed a two-orbital tight-binding model, where boron states at the Fermi level are integrated out. Using this model, we identify an extended-$s$ symmetry as the leading pairing instability. If confirmed, this will be the first reported case of superconductivity driven by altermagnetic spin fluctuations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that superconductivity in MnB4 (Tc = 14 K at 158 GPa) is driven by altermagnetic spin fluctuations rather than conventional electron-phonon coupling, identifying extended-s symmetry as the pairing state - potentially the first confirmed case of altermagnetic fluctuation-driven superconductivity.
AI.motivation: Conventional electron-phonon coupling calculations predict Tc < 1 K for MnB4, contradicting experimental observations of Tc reaching 14 K. This discrepancy suggests an unconventional pairing mechanism is responsible for the superconductivity.
AI.method: The authors used density-functional theory (DFT) calculations to identify that MnB4 is near an altermagnetic instability. They constructed a two-orbital tight-binding model by integrating out boron states at the Fermi level to analyze the pairing symmetry.
AI.result: Extended-s symmetry was identified as the leading pairing instability in MnB4, indicating superconductivity driven by altermagnetic spin fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: If confirmed experimentally, this would represent the first reported case of superconductivity driven by altermagnetic spin fluctuations, establishing a new paradigm for unconventional superconductivity mechanisms.

title: Giant spontaneous Kerr effect reveals the defect origin of macroscopic time-reversal symmetry breaking in altermagnetic MnTe
summary: Altermagnetism, a recently identified third class of collinear magnetism with spin-split bands and vanishing net magnetization, has emerged in hexagonal \alphaMnTe{} and is regarded as a promising platform for ultrafast, stray-field-free spintronics and for optical readout of spin order at telecommunication wavelengths. Whether the macroscopic symmetry-breaking signatures reported in MnTe, a spontaneous Hall effect and a tiny ``gossamer'' remanent moment, reflect the ideal altermagnetic order or are activated by defects remains an open question. Here we report giant spontaneous Kerr rotations of up to $\pm 1500\microrad$ in \alphaMnTe{} single crystals at the telecommunication wavelength of $1550\,\mathrm{nm}$, onsetting precisely at the Néel temperature $\TN = 307\,\mathrm{K}$. In contrast, a stoichiometric insulating \alphaMnTe{} thin film shows no detectable signal. The bulk--film contrast identifies carrier self-doping, rather than the ideal altermagnetic order, as the source of macroscopic magneto-optical response, establishing telecom-wavelength Kerr imaging as a practical readout for altermagnetic spintronics.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether the macroscopic symmetry-breaking signatures (spontaneous Hall effect and remanent moment) in αMnTe altermagnet originate from ideal altermagnetic order or from defects. Using telecom-wavelength (1550nm) Kerr rotation imaging, they discovered giant spontaneous Kerr rotations (±1500 μrad) in MnTe single crystals onsetting at TN=307K, but no detectable signal in stoichiometric insulating thin films. This bulk-film contrast reveals that carrier self-doping, not ideal altermagnetic order, is the source of the magneto-optical response.
AI.motivation: The fundamental question driving this research is whether the observed macroscopic symmetry-breaking signatures in αMnTe (spontaneous Hall effect and tiny "gossamer" remanent moment) reflect ideal altermagnetic order or are activated by defects. This is critical for developing altermagnetic spintronics applications.
AI.method: The researchers used telecom-wavelength (1550nm) Kerr rotation imaging to probe αMnTe single crystals and compared them with stoichiometric insulating αMnTe thin films. They measured the magneto-optical response at the telecommunication wavelength and compared bulk crystals with thin films.
AI.result: Giant spontaneous Kerr rotations of up to ±1500 μrad were observed in αMnTe single crystals at 1550nm, onsetting precisely at the Néel temperature TN=307K. In contrast, stoichiometric insulating αMnTe thin films showed no detectable signal. The bulk-film contrast indicates that carrier self-doping (not ideal altermagnetic order) is the source of the macroscopic magneto-optical response.
AI.conclusion: Carrier self-doping, rather than ideal altermagnetic order, is responsible for the macroscopic magneto-optical response in MnTe. This finding establishes telecom-wavelength Kerr imaging as a practical readout method for altermagnetic spintronics applications.

title: Predicting Scale-Up of Metal-Organic Framework Syntheses with Large Language Models
summary: Scalable synthesis remains the gate between MOF discovery and industrial deployment, as scale-up know-how is fragmented across disparate reports. We introduce ESU-MOF, a literature-mined dataset and a positive-unlabeled learning strategy that fine-tunes large language models to predict scalability potential with 91.4% accuracy, enabling rapid data-driven triage for industrial MOF discovery.

title: Two-gap to Single-gap Transition and Two-dome-like Superconductivity in Alkali-Metal Intercalated Bilayer PdTe2
summary: PdTe2 has been synthesized with controllable thickness down to the monolayer limit. Based on first-principles calculations within the fully anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg framework, this work reveals that alkali-metal intercalation markedly enhances the weak superconductivity of bilayer PdTe2, boosting the transition temperature from 1.4 K to 5.0 -13.5 K and yielding a two-dome-like evolution of Tc. Rubidium intercalation induces the highest Tc of 13.5 K, which can be further increased to 14.5 K under biaxial tensile strain. The strain-dependent evolution of Tc also exhibits a two-dome-like behavior, reflecting the interplay between strain-induced band structure modifications and electron-phonon coupling (EPC). Moreover, a systematic correlation is identified between interlayer interaction and superconducting gap. Lithium intercalation induces a distinct two-gap state, whereas intercalants with larger atomic radii (Na, K, Rb, and Cs) drive the system into a single-gap character. The two-gap to single-gap transition originates from the modulation of interlayer coupling through intercalation-induced interlayer expansion. In addition, pristine and Li/Na-intercalated bilayers exhibit nontrivial band topology, suggesting that layered PdTe2 provides a promising platform for realizing the coexistence of superconductivity and nontrivial topology. These results provide detailed anisotropic insights into EPC and offer viable pathways for enhancing Tc and achieving diverse properties in layered PdTe2 systems.

AI.tldr: This theoretical study demonstrates that alkali-metal intercalation in bilayer PdTe2 significantly enhances superconductivity, increasing Tc from 1.4 K up to 14.5 K under optimal conditions. The work reveals a two-dome behavior in Tc evolution and identifies a transition from two-gap to single-gap superconductivity depending on intercalant size, while also showing potential for coexisting superconductivity and nontrivial topology.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enhance the weak superconductivity of bilayer PdTe2 (Tc = 1.4 K) through chemical intercalation and understand the underlying mechanisms, while exploring the relationship between interlayer interactions, electron-phonon coupling, and superconducting properties in layered materials.
AI.method: First-principles calculations within the fully anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg framework were employed to study electron-phonon coupling and superconductivity. This approach allows for detailed investigation of anisotropic superconducting properties and strain effects on the electronic structure.
AI.result: Alkali-metal intercalation dramatically enhances Tc from 1.4 K to 5.0-13.5 K, with rubidium achieving the highest Tc of 13.5 K. Biaxial tensile strain further increases Tc to 14.5 K. Both intercalation and strain exhibit two-dome-like Tc evolution. A systematic correlation was found between interlayer interaction and superconducting gap structure: Li intercalation yields a two-gap state, while larger atoms (Na, K, Rb, Cs) result in single-gap behavior. The two-gap to single-gap transition originates from intercalation-induced interlayer expansion. Pristine and Li/Na-intercalated bilayers exhibit nontrivial band topology, suggesting potential for coexisting superconductivity and topology.
AI.conclusion: Layered PdTe2 is a promising platform for enhancing Tc through alkali-metal intercalation and strain engineering. The work provides detailed anisotropic insights into electron-phonon coupling and offers viable pathways for achieving diverse properties, including the coexistence of superconductivity and nontrivial topology in 2D materials.

title: Studying 3D O(N) Surface CFT on the Fuzzy Sphere
summary: Boundary conformal field theory (BCFT) provides a universal framework for critical phenomena in the presence of boundaries. We determine BCFT data for the normal and ordinary boundary universality classes of the $1+1$-dimensional boundaries of the $2+1$-dimensional $O(2)$ and $O(3)$ Wilson-Fisher fixed points, realized microscopically by a bilayer Heisenberg model on the fuzzy sphere. Using the fuzzy-sphere state-operator correspondence, we obtain boundary operator spectra, identify low-lying boundary primary operators, extract operator-product-expansion (OPE) data, and estimate the boundary central charges for both boundary conditions. For the normal boundary condition, the universal amplitudes $a_σ$ and $b_t$ extracted from one- and two-point functions agree quantitatively with Monte Carlo benchmarks where available. For both $N=2$ and $N=3$, we find a positive extraordinary-log exponent $α$, providing independent microscopic evidence for extraordinary-log boundary criticality. Our results extend fuzzy-sphere BCFT spectroscopy beyond the Ising universality class to continuous $O(N)$ symmetry.

AI.tldr: This paper applies Boundary Conformal Field Theory (BCFT) to study critical phenomena in bilayer Heisenberg models on the fuzzy sphere, determining boundary operator spectra, OPE data, and boundary central charges for O(2) and O(3) Wilson-Fisher fixed points, with results confirming Monte Carlo benchmarks and providing evidence for extraordinary-log boundary criticality.
AI.motivation: The authors seek to determine BCFT data for the normal and ordinary boundary universality classes of 1+1-dimensional boundaries of 2+1-dimensional O(2) and O(3) Wilson-Fisher fixed points, which provide a universal framework for critical phenomena in the presence of boundaries.
AI.method: The authors use the fuzzy-sphere state-operator correspondence to obtain boundary operator spectra, identify low-lying boundary primary operators, extract operator-product-expansion (OPE) data, and estimate boundary central charges for both boundary conditions.
AI.result: For the normal boundary condition, the universal amplitudes a_σ and b_t extracted from one- and two-point functions agree quantitatively with Monte Carlo benchmarks. For both N=2 and N=3, a positive extraordinary-log exponent α is found, providing independent microscopic evidence for extraordinary-log boundary criticality.
AI.conclusion: The results extend fuzzy-sphere BCFT spectroscopy beyond the Ising universality class to continuous O(N) symmetry, providing important boundary critical behavior data for O(N) models with N≥2.

title: Evolution of the Saddle Point in Antimony Telluride Homologous Superlattices
summary: Combining topological insulators with topological semimetals in the form of homologous superlattices is a promising approach for generating correlated quantum matter based upon Fermi level alignment with band extrema. For antimony telluride, a saddle point is predicted to occur at the M-point, while antimonene layering is predicted to move the M-point valence band towards the Fermi level. To date, the predicted saddle point at the M-point has not yet been demonstrated, and studies of antimony telluride homologous superlattices have been limited to one or two layers of antimonene added to antimony telluride. Here, we present scanning tunneling spectroscopy and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy studies of a series of antimony telluride homologous superlattices with two to four layers of antimonene. In addition to demonstrating the presence of a saddle point and associated van Hove singularity near the M-point, we identify the key role of Sb and Te $p_z$ orbital hybridization in driving the van Hove singularity toward the Fermi level.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates antimony telluride homologous superlattices with 2-4 layers of antimonene, demonstrating a predicted saddle point at the M-point and associated van Hove singularity, identifying the key role of Sb and Te p_z orbital hybridization in driving the van Hove singularity toward the Fermi level.
AI.motivation: The predicted saddle point at the M-point in antimony telluride has not been previously demonstrated, and studies have been limited to only one or two layers of antimonene. This work aims to explore longer superlattice sequences and verify the saddle point phenomenon.
AI.method: Scanning tunneling spectroscopy and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy were used to study a series of antimony telluride homologous superlattices with two to four layers of antimonene.
AI.result: The study demonstrates the presence of a saddle point and associated van Hove singularity near the M-point, and identifies the key role of Sb and Te p_z orbital hybridization in driving the van Hove singularity toward the Fermi level.
AI.conclusion: The findings confirm the predicted saddle point at the M-point and provide insight into the orbital mechanisms underlying the electronic properties of these homologous superlattices, which is valuable for generating correlated quantum matter based on Fermi level alignment with band extrema.

title: Effect of Mn Substitution on Superconductivity in PrFeAs(O,F): Role of Magnetic Impurities
summary: We investigate Mn substitution at the Fe site in PrFe1-xMnxAsO0.7F0.3 (0 to 0.1) using structural, Raman, density functional theory (DFT), transport, and magnetic measurements. X-ray diffraction and Raman analyses confirm preferential Mn incorporation into the FeAs planes, accompanied by lattice expansion and suppression of Fe-related vibrational modes. Electrical transport reveals a systematic decrease of the superconducting transition temperature from 48 K (x = 0) to complete suppression at x = 0.1, together with low-temperature resistivity upturns evolving toward insulating-like behavior. Magnetization and magnetotransport measurements show degradation of superconducting coherence, critical current density, upper critical field, and vortex activation energy with increasing Mn content. The results demonstrate that Mn acts as an efficient magnetic impurity, strongly perturbing the electronic and magnetic environment of the FeAs layers. Comparative analysis indicates relatively enhanced robustness of superconductivity in the Pr-based system, highlighting the role of rare-earth-dependent electronic correlations in impurity effects.

AI.tldr: Study of Mn substitution in PrFeAsO superconductor reveals that Mn acts as an efficient magnetic impurity that progressively suppresses superconductivity from Tc=48K to zero at x=0.1, while the Pr-based system shows relatively enhanced robustness compared to other iron-based superconductors.
AI.motivation: Investigate the effects of Mn magnetic impurity substitution at the Fe site in PrFeAsO iron-based superconductor, to understand how magnetic impurities perturb the electronic and magnetic environment of FeAs layers and affect superconductivity.
AI.method: X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, DFT calculations, electrical transport measurements, magnetization and magnetotransport measurements on PrFe1-xMnxAsO0.7F0.3 (x = 0 to 0.1) samples
AI.result: Mn preferentially incorporates into FeAs planes, causing lattice expansion and suppression of Fe-related vibrational modes. Superconducting transition temperature Tc systematically decreases from 48 K (x=0) to complete suppression at x=0.1. Low-temperature resistivity upturns evolve toward insulating-like behavior. Superconducting coherence, critical current density, upper critical field, and vortex activation energy all degrade with increasing Mn content.
AI.conclusion: Mn substitution in PrFeAsO introduces strong magnetic pair-breaking effects, ultimately suppressing superconductivity. The Pr-based system exhibits relatively enhanced robustness compared to other iron-based superconductors, suggesting that rare-earth-dependent electronic correlations play a protective role against impurity effects. These findings highlight the delicate nature of superconductivity in iron-based materials and the importance of magnetic impurity sensitivity.

title: BCS-BEC crossover of polaritonic condensates in mass-imbalanced semimetal/semiconductor microcavities
summary: The impacts of the mass imbalance and Coulomb interaction on the complex phase structures of the polaritonic condensates and their Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS)--Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) crossover in semiconductor and semimetal microcavities are investigated. In the framework of the unrestricted Hartree-Fock approximation, a two-band electron-hole model involving photon mode is analyzed by treating Coulomb attraction and light-matter coupling on equal footing. The single-particle spectral functions and the luminescence properties are then examined. In the semiconducting regime, a positive band gap stabilizes tightly bound excitons and yields predominantly BEC-type excitoniclike polaritonic condensates at low density, while increasing excitation density and reducing mass imbalance drives a continuous crossover toward BCS-type pairing with intermediate and photoniclike polaritonic character. In contrast, the semimetallic regime favors itinerant electron-hole pairing, with BCS-type condensates dominating and BEC excitoniclike coherence emerging only at sufficiently strong Coulomb interaction and large mass imbalance situations. The evolution of luminescence spectra provides clear spectroscopic signatures of these crossover phenomena, offering a unified framework for understanding and controlling polaritonic condensates in microcavity systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the interplay between mass imbalance and Coulomb interaction in determining the phase structure of polaritonic condensates in semiconductor and semimetal microcavities, focusing on the BCS-BEC crossover behavior and providing spectroscopic signatures through luminescence measurements.
AI.motivation: Understanding and controlling polaritonic condensates requires unified theoretical framework that treats Coulomb attraction and light-matter coupling on equal footing—particularly for predicting crossover behavior from excitonic BEC to BCS-type pairing in different material regimes.
AI.method: The authors employ unrestricted Hartree-Fock approximation to analyze a two-band electron-hole model with photon mode, examining single-particle spectral functions and luminescence properties across semiconductor and semimetallic regimes.
AI.result: In semiconductors: positive band gap stabilizes excitons → BEC-type polaritonic condensates at low density; increasing density or reducing mass imbalance drives crossover to BCS-type pairing. In semimetals: BCS-type condensates dominate; strong Coulomb interaction and large mass imbalance enable BEC excitoniclike coherence. Luminescence spectra provide clear spectroscopic signatures of these crossover phenomena.
AI.conclusion: This work offers a unified framework for understanding and controlling polaritonic condensates in microcavity systems, with clear spectroscopic signatures enabling experimental verification of BCS-BEC crossover phenomena.

title: Expanding the extreme-k dielectric materials space through physics-validated generative reasoning
summary: The most technologically consequential materials are often the rarest: they occupy narrow regions of chemical space, obey competing physical constraints, and appear only sparsely in existing databases. High-kappa dielectrics, high-Tc superconductors, and ferromagnetic insulators are to name a few. This scarcity fundamentally limits today's data-driven materials discovery, where machine-learning models excel at interpolation but struggle to generate genuinely new candidates. Here, we introduce DielecMIND, an artificial intelligence framework that reframes materials discovery as a reasoning-driven exploration instead of a database-screening problem. Using high-kappa dielectrics as a data-scarce and technologically stringent test case, DielecMIND combines large-language-model hypothesis generation for the first time with physics validated first-principles calculation to navigate chemical space beyond known compounds. Prior to our work, only 14 experimentally or computationally validated materials with kappa > 150 were known. Our framework discovers and validates 5 new such compounds, expanding this rare-materials class by a remarkable = 35% in a single study. Among them, we find that Ba2TiHfO6 exhibits a dielectric constant of 637, minimal loss at low optical frequencies, and stability up to 800 K. Beyond dielectrics, this work demonstrates a new paradigm for artificial-intelligence-guided discovery: one that generates a small number of physically grounded, experimentally plausible candidates yet measurably expands sparsely populated functional materials spaces. Thus, DielecMIND points toward a general strategy for discovering rare, high-impact functional materials where data scarcity has long constrained progress.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DielecMIND, an AI framework that combines large-language-model hypothesis generation with physics-validated first-principles calculations to discover high-kappa dielectric materials - a rare and technologically important class of materials. The framework successfully discovered 5 new compounds with kappa > 150, expanding the known class by 35%.
AI.motivation: The most technologically consequential materials (high-kappa dielectrics, high-Tc superconductors, ferromagnetic insulators) are often the rarest, occupying narrow regions of chemical space. This scarcity fundamentally limits data-driven materials discovery, where machine-learning models excel at interpolation but struggle to generate genuinely new candidates beyond known compounds.
AI.method: DielecMIND combines large-language-model (LLM) hypothesis generation with physics-validated first-principles calculation to navigate chemical space beyond known compounds. This reframes materials discovery as reasoning-driven exploration instead of database-screening.
AI.result: Prior to this work, only 14 experimentally or computationally validated materials with kappa > 150 were known. The framework discovers and validates 5 new such compounds, expanding this rare-materials class by 35%. Among the new discoveries, Ba2TiHfO6 exhibits a dielectric constant of 637, minimal loss at low optical frequencies, and stability up to 800 K.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates a new paradigm for AI-guided discovery: one that generates a small number of physically grounded, experimentally plausible candidates yet measurably expands sparsely populated functional materials spaces. DielecMIND points toward a general strategy for discovering rare, high-impact functional materials where data scarcity has long constrained progress.

title: $3d_{z^2}$ orbital delocalization and magnetic collapse in superconducting (La,Pr)$_3$Ni$_2$O$_{7-δ}$ films
summary: The recent discovery of Ruddlesden-Popper (RP) nickelate thin-film superconductors has opened a new frontier in unconventional superconductivity. Its realization requires both compressive epitaxial strain and highly oxidative growth conditions, yet the microscopic pathway from the parent phase to the superconducting phase remains elusive. Here, X-ray absorption spectroscopy and resonant inelastic X-ray scattering are employed to track this evolution by independently tuning strain and oxygen content in (La,Pr)$_3$Ni$_2$O$_{7-δ}$ thin films. We uncover a remarkable two-step evolution. First, signatures of delocalization emerge in the same way upon two independent tunings: spectral weight transfers from an upper Hubbard-like peak to the hole-like peak associated with the O $2p_z$ state, and in parallel, the initially localized Ni $3d_{z^2}$ orbital becomes more itinerant, accompanied by the broadening and weakening of $dd$ orbital excitations. Second, as itinerancy increases, long-range spin-density-wave (SDW) order is suppressed in both intensity and correlation length, indicating direct competition with superconductivity. Yet, short-range magnons persist: they become damped, but their bandwidth remains unchanged. Our results paint a coherent picture in which both strain and oxygenation drive the RP bilayer nickelates towards superconducting instability, where the O $2p_z$ and Ni $3d_{z^2}$ orbitals become delocalized. Concomitantly, the long-range magnetic order loses coherence and is suppressed. These findings establish an orbital-selective route to RP nickelate superconductivity, in which the delocalization of the interlayer $3d_{z^2}$-$2p_z$-$3d_{z^2}$ molecular orbital and the robust short-range magnons upon the melting of SDW order are prerequisites, providing strong constraints for theory and a roadmap for designing nickelate superconductors.

AI.tldr: This study reveals a two-step evolution pathway to superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelate thin films, where strain and oxygenation drive orbital delocalization (Ni 3dz2 and O 2pz) while suppressing long-range SDW order but preserving short-range magnons.
AI.motivation: The discovery of RP nickelate superconductors requires compressive epitaxial strain and highly oxidative conditions, but the microscopic pathway from parent phase to superconducting phase remained unclear.
AI.method: X-ray absorption spectroscopy and resonant inelastic X-ray scattering were used to track the evolution by independently tuning strain and oxygen content in (La,Pr)3Ni2O7-δ thin films.
AI.result: A two-step evolution was discovered: (1) Delocalization emerges similarly from both strain and oxygen tuning - spectral weight transfers from upper Hubbard-like peak to O 2pz hole-like peak, Ni 3dz2 orbital becomes more itinerant, dd excitations broaden and weaken; (2) Long-range SDW order is suppressed in intensity and correlation length, while short-range magnons persist (damped but bandwidth unchanged).
AI.conclusion: An orbital-selective route to RP nickelate superconductivity is established, where delocalization of the interlayer 3dz2-2pz-3dz2 molecular orbital and robust short-range magnons upon melting of SDW order are prerequisites for superconductivity, providing constraints for theory and a roadmap for designing nickelate superconductors.

title: Condensate states in Fermi and Bose-Hubbard ladders
summary: Although neither hardcore bosons nor fermions can occupy the same single-site state, they still obey different statistics, resulting in distinct many-particle quantum states, such as condensate states versus Fermi-liquid states. However, when only pair states are considered, the two can take the same form, since a local hardcore Bose pair and a Fermi pair obey the same statistics. In this work we demonstrate this by studying both Fermi and Bose extended Hubbard ladders, which can be realized experimentally in synthetic atomic ladders. A set of exact condensate-pair eigenstates for the Fermi ladder is constructed under SU(2) symmetry and can then be obtained by the spectrum generating algebra. The corresponding hardcore boson counterpart can be simply obtained by replacing fermionic operators with hardcore bosonic ones. Nevertheless, the boson-pair eigenstates are associated not with symmetry but with the restricted spectrum generating algebra. We also investigate the effect of next-nearest-neighbor hopping on the condensate states through numerical simulations of the dynamic response. The conclusions can be extended to a two-layer system. Our result reveals not only the resemblance of fermions to hardcore bosons, but also a possible mechanism of Hilbert-space fragmentation.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that hardcore bosons and fermions can exhibit identical pair-state forms despite different single-particle statistics, by constructing exact condensate-pair eigenstates for both Fermi and Bose extended Hubbard ladders using SU(2) symmetry and spectrum generating algebra. The results reveal a fundamental connection between these particle types and suggest a mechanism for Hilbert-space fragmentation.
AI.motivation: Despite different statistics at single-particle level, a local hardcore Bose pair and a Fermi pair obey the same statistics, suggesting their many-particle quantum states could take identical forms. This work aims to demonstrate this correspondence using extended Hubbard ladder systems realizable in synthetic atomic ladders.
AI.method: Construct exact condensate-pair eigenstates under SU(2) symmetry for the Fermi ladder via spectrum generating algebra; replace fermionic operators with hardcore bosonic ones for the boson counterpart; perform numerical simulations to investigate next-nearest-neighbor hopping effects on dynamic response.
AI.result: Exact condensate-pair eigenstates are constructed for both Fermi and Bose ladders; the boson-pair eigenstates are found to be associated with restricted spectrum generating algebra rather than symmetry, distinct from their fermionic counterparts.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate both the mathematical resemblance between fermions and hardcore bosons at the pair level and reveal a possible mechanism of Hilbert-space fragmentation, with conclusions extendable to two-layer systems.

title: Accelerating point defect simulations using data-driven and machine learning approaches
comment: Part of the MRS Bulletin '100 Years of Point Defects' Special Collection
summary: Point defects in solid-state materials are now routinely simulated using large supercell structures, requiring efficient quantum mechanical solutions. Data-driven and machine learning (ML) models trained on computational data can enable rapid defect property predictions and high-throughput screening. In this article, we provide an overview of prominent efforts to accelerate defect simulations using these approaches. We begin by discussing the motivations for data-driven techniques in defect modeling, and describe efforts over the past decade to use descriptor-based models for rapid screening of defect properties -- most notably in oxides. In particular, we discuss case studies where surrogate models and interatomic potentials were trained on density functional theory (DFT) data, leading to predictions with quantum-mechanical accuracies at a fraction of the cost. In addition to geometry relaxation and formation energy predictions, these interatomic potentials are capable of predicting phonon modes and vibrational free energies to yield defect energetics at finite temperatures -- representing a key frontier for computational defect research. We finish with a discussion on how to connect these approaches and their outputs with experimental data, and provide an outlook on this burgeoning sub-field.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews data-driven and machine learning approaches for accelerating point defect simulations in solid-state materials, including descriptor-based models and interatomic potentials trained on DFT data for rapid defect property predictions and high-throughput screening.
AI.motivation: Point defects in solid-state materials are simulated using large supercell structures requiring efficient quantum mechanical solutions. Data-driven and ML models are needed to enable rapid defect property predictions and high-throughput screening.
AI.method: The article discusses descriptor-based models, surrogate models, and interatomic potentials trained on density functional theory (DFT) data. These models predict geometry relaxation, formation energy, phonon modes, and vibrational free energies.
AI.result: The approaches achieve quantum-mechanical accuracy at a fraction of the computational cost, enabling predictions of defect energetics at finite temperatures.
AI.conclusion: The field represents a key frontier for computational defect research, with potential for connecting simulation outputs with experimental data. It is a burgeoning sub-field with opportunities for future development.

title: $η$-pairing in a two-band model of spinless fermions
summary: We study the two-band model of spinless fermions in which itinerant fermions interact with localized fermions through the two-particle hybridization. In 1D version, the model has exact solution using the Bethe ansatz. It has been shown that accounting for two-particle hybridization reduces the repulsive interaction between itinerant fermions. In the case of strong interaction, the effective interaction between itinerant fermions is attractive, and $η$-pairing of spinless fermions is realized. The proposed pairing mechanism via two-particle hybridization can lead to $p$-superconducting states with $η$-pairing. $η$-pairing of spinless fermions could explain the phenomenon of high-temperature superconductivity experimentally observed in hydrogen-rich materials at high pressures.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates a two-band model of spinless fermions with two-particle hybridization, showing that this interaction can reduce repulsive interactions between itinerant fermions and even become attractive in the strong coupling limit, enabling η-pairing and p-superconducting states that may explain high-temperature superconductivity in hydrogen-rich materials under high pressure.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the mechanism behind high-temperature superconductivity observed in hydrogen-rich materials at high pressures, and to explore how two-particle hybridization affects fermion interactions in the two-band model.
AI.method: The study uses the Bethe ansatz method to solve the 1D version of the two-band spinless fermion model exactly, analyzing the effective interaction between itinerant fermions under two-particle hybridization.
AI.result: Two-particle hybridization reduces repulsive interaction between itinerant fermions. In the strong interaction regime, the effective interaction becomes attractive, enabling η-pairing of spinless fermions and leading to p-superconducting states.
AI.conclusion: The proposed η-pairing mechanism via two-particle hybridization provides a plausible explanation for high-temperature superconductivity in hydrogen-rich materials under high pressure.

title: Generative Discovery of Magnetic Insulators under Competing Physical Constraints
summary: Discovering materials that must simultaneously satisfy multiple competing constraints remains a central challenge in computational materials design, particularly in data-scarce regimes where conventional data-driven approaches are least effective. Magnetic insulators represent a stringent example: the electronic conditions that favor magnetic order often also promote metallicity, while insulating behavior suppresses the interactions that stabilize magnetism. As a result, experimentally viable magnetic insulators are rare and difficult to identify through conventional screening. Here, we introduce MagMatLLM, a constraint-guided generative discovery framework that integrates language-model-based crystal generation with evolutionary selection, surrogate screening, and first-principles validation to target simultaneous stability, magnetism, and insulating behavior. Unlike stability-first approaches, the framework enforces functional constraints during generation and selection, steering the search toward sparsely populated regions of materials space defined by competing physical requirements. Using this workflow, we identify twelve previously unreported candidate magnetic insulators, including Tm$_4$Co$_2$Cr$_2$O$_{12}$ and Cr$_4$Nb$_2$O$_{12}$. Of these, ten are dynamically stable by phonon analysis and exhibit finite band gaps and nonzero magnetic moments in spin-polarized density functional theory calculations. Beyond the specific compounds identified here, this work establishes a general constraint-guided paradigm for multi-objective materials discovery in sparse chemical spaces and provides a transferable strategy for the design of quantum materials under competing physical constraints.

AI.tldr: MagMatLLM is a constraint-guided generative framework combining language models, evolutionary selection, and DFT validation to discover magnetic insulators—a rare class of materials where magnetic order and insulating behavior compete. The approach enforces functional constraints during generation (rather than after) to explore sparse regions of materials space. Twelve new candidates were found, with ten validated computationally.
AI.motivation: Magnetic insulators are rare and difficult to identify because the electronic conditions favoring magnetic order typically also promote metallicity, while insulating behavior suppresses interactions that stabilize magnetism. Conventional data-driven screening approaches are ineffective in this data-scarce regime where materials must satisfy multiple competing constraints simultaneously.
AI.method: MagMatLLM integrates language-model-based crystal generation with evolutionary selection, surrogate screening, and first-principles validation. Unlike stability-first approaches, functional constraints (stability, magnetism, insulating behavior) are enforced during generation and selection to steer searches toward sparsely populated regions defined by competing physical requirements.
AI.result: 12 previously unreported candidate magnetic insulators were identified (e.g., Tm₄Co₂Cr₂O₁₂, Cr₄Nb₂O₁₂). Ten candidates are dynamically stable by phonon analysis and exhibit finite band gaps with nonzero magnetic moments in spin-polarized DFT calculations.
AI.conclusion: MagMatLLM establishes a general constraint-guided paradigm for multi-objective materials discovery in sparse chemical spaces, providing a transferable strategy for quantum materials design under competing physical constraints. The framework identified 12 new candidate magnetic insulators, with 10 validated through DFT calculations as dynamically stable with finite band gaps and nonzero magnetic moments.

title: Intertwined charge density wave, tunable anti-dome superconductivity, and topological states in kagome metal VSn
summary: These years, kagome materials with 1:1 stoichiometry have garnered increasing attention, among which FeSn, CoSn, and FeGe have been the focus of current studies. However, all of them are antiferromagnetic, thereby hindering the observation of superconductivity and other novel physical properties. Here, we predict a novel 1:1 kagome metal VSn, which is an intrinsic charge density wave (CDW) material. Interestingly, with increasing pressure or doping concentration, the CDW order is progressively suppressed, followed by the emergence of superconductivity characterized by a non-monotonic transition temperature that exhibits a rare anti-dome-shaped dependence. Above a critical threshold, a reentrance of the CDW phase occurs. The anti-dome superconductivity originates from the first hardening and then softening of phonon modes, together with band reconstruction. Crucially, VSn retains nontrivial topological properties across the entire superconducting regime, a feature of paramount importance for realizing robust topological superconductivity. These intertwined CDW, superconductivity, and topological phenomena elucidate the correlations among multiple quantum states in VSn. Therefore, this research paves the way for for designing 1:1 kagome superconducting topological metals and establishes a platform for exploring the interplay of multiple phases in kagome systems.

AI.tldr: A novel 1:1 kagome metal VSn is predicted to exhibit intertwined charge density wave (CDW), superconductivity with anti-dome behavior, and robust nontrivial topological properties, providing a promising platform for exploring multiple quantum phases in kagome systems.
AI.motivation: Existing 1:1 kagome materials (FeSn, CoSn, FeGe) are antiferromagnetic and thus limit the observation of superconductivity and novel quantum states. There is a need to identify a kagome material that can exhibit superconductivity while retaining nontrivial topological properties.
AI.method: The authors employ first-principles calculations, likely based on density functional theory (DFT), to predict the properties of VSn under varying pressures and doping concentrations. They analyze phonon dispersion, band structure reconstruction, and topological invariants across different regimes.
AI.result: VSn is predicted as an intrinsic CDW material. Under pressure or doping, the CDW order is progressively suppressed, leading to superconductivity with a non-monotonic, anti-dome-shaped transition temperature. Above a critical threshold, CDW reenters. The superconductivity originates from phonon hardening followed by softening and band reconstruction. Importantly, VSn retains nontrivial topological properties throughout the superconducting regime.
AI.conclusion: VSn is identified as a promising 1:1 kagome metal that hosts intertwined CDW, superconductivity, and nontrivial topological phases, serving as an ideal platform for exploring quantum phase competition and a blueprint for designing superconducting topological metals in kagome systems.

title: Pressure-Tuned Competing Electronic States in Layered Tellurides
summary: Layered transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) host competing electronic states that can be tuned by external perturbations, providing a platform to explore the interplay between disorder, electronic structure, and quantum transport. Here we investigate magnetotransport in bulk semiconducting 2H-MoTe2 under hydrostatic pressure. At ambient pressure, transport evolves from high-temperature metallic behavior into activated conduction and ultimately a strongly localized variable-range hopping regime, accompanied by a pronounced magnetotransport anomaly near 45 K and large, nonsaturating magnetoresistance extending up to an unprecedented field of 60 T in semiconducting 2H-MoTe2. Under compression to 15.6 GPa, the insulating state is rapidly suppressed and a low-resistivity regime emerges in which quantum interference dominates, exhibiting a crossover from weak antilocalization (WAL) to weak localization (WL) at low temperatures. A physically motivated phenomenological description captures the magnetoresistance across these regimes and yields a characteristic electronic length scale that remains comparable across the localized and quantum-interference regimes. First-principles calculations reveal a continuous pressure-driven collapse of the bandgap into a semimetallic electronic structure. These results establish a unified picture of pressure-tuned transport spanning hopping and quantum-coherent regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates magnetotransport properties of bulk semiconducting 2H-MoTe2 under hydrostatic pressure, revealing a transition from activated conduction and variable-range hopping at ambient pressure to quantum-coherent transport with weak localization-antilocalization crossover under high pressure, accompanied by a pressure-driven bandgap collapse to semimetallic state.
AI.motivation: Layered transition-metal dichalcogenides host competing electronic states tunable by external perturbations, providing a platform to explore the interplay between disorder, electronic structure, and quantum transport. The motivation is to understand how hydrostatic pressure affects the transport mechanisms and quantum interference effects in these materials.
AI.method: The researchers performed magnetotransport measurements on bulk 2H-MoTe2 under hydrostatic pressure up to 15.6 GPa, using fields up to 60 T. They also conducted first-principles calculations to study the electronic structure evolution under pressure.
AI.result: At ambient pressure, transport evolves from high-temperature metallic behavior into activated conduction and variable-range hopping, with a magnetotransport anomaly near 45 K and large nonsaturating magnetoresistance up to 60 T. Under compression to 15.6 GPa, the insulating state is suppressed, revealing a low-resistivity regime with quantum interference dominated transport, showing crossover from weak antilocalization (WAL) to weak localization (WL) at low temperatures. First-principles calculations reveal a continuous pressure-driven collapse of the bandgap into a semimetallic electronic structure.
AI.conclusion: The results establish a unified picture of pressure-tuned transport spanning hopping and quantum-coherent regimes in 2H-MoTe2, with a characteristic electronic length scale remaining comparable across localized and quantum-interference regimes.

title: Neutron and X-ray Diffraction Reveal the Limits of Long-Range Machine Learning Potentials for Medium-Range Order in Silica Glass
summary: Glassy silica is a foundational material in optics and electronics, yet accurately predicting its medium-range order (MRO) remains a major challenge for machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs). While local MLIPs reproduce the short-range SiO4 tetrahedral network well, it remains unclear whether locality alone is sufficient to recover the first sharp diffraction peak (FSDP), the principal experimental signature of MRO. Here, we combine neutron and X-ray diffraction measurements with large-scale molecular dynamics driven by two MACE-based models: a short-range (SR) potential and a long-range (LR) extension incorporating reciprocal-space gated attention. The SR model systematically over-structures the network, producing an overly intense FSDP in both the liquid and glassy states. Incorporating long-range interactions improves agreement with experiment for the liquid structure by reducing this excess ordering, but the LR model still fails to recover the experimental amorphous MRO after quenching. Ring-statistics and bond-angle analyses reveal that SR model exhibits an artificially narrow distribution dominated by six-membered rings, while the LR model produces a broader but still biased ring population. Despite preserving the correct tetrahedral geometry, both models show limited variability in Si-O-Si angles, indicating constrained network flexibility. These structural signatures demonstrate that both models retain excessive memory of the parent liquid network, leading to kinetically trapped and nonphysical medium-range configurations during vitrification. These results show that explicit long-range interactions are necessary but not sufficient for predictive modelling of disordered silica and suggest that accurate MRO further requires training data and sampling strategies that adequately represent the liquid-to-glass transition.

AI.tldr: This study investigates whether machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) can capture medium-range order (MRO) in glassy silica, specifically the first sharp diffraction peak (FSDP). Using MACE-based models with short-range (SR) and long-range (LR) interactions, combined with neutron/X-ray diffraction, the research reveals that both models over-structure the network and fail to recover experimental amorphous MRO after quenching, highlighting that long-range interactions alone are insufficient.
AI.motivation: Glassy silica is fundamental in optics and electronics, but predicting its medium-range order (MRO) remains challenging for MLIPs. While local MLIPs reproduce short-range SiO4 tetrahedral networks well, it was unclear whether locality alone could recover the FSDP—the key experimental signature of MRO. This study addresses this gap by testing whether long-range interactions improve MRO prediction.
AI.method: The researchers combined neutron and X-ray diffraction measurements with large-scale molecular dynamics using two MACE-based models: a short-range (SR) potential and a long-range (LR) extension incorporating reciprocal-space gated attention. They analyzed ring-statistics, bond-angle distributions, and FSDP intensity in both liquid and glassy states.
AI.result: The SR model systematically over-structures the network, producing an overly intense FSDP in both states. The LR model improves liquid structure agreement but still fails to recover experimental amorphous MRO after quenching. Both models show narrow ring distributions (SR: six-membered rings; LR: broader but biased) and limited Si-O-Si angle variability. Both retain excessive memory of the parent liquid network, leading to kinetically trapped nonphysical configurations.
AI.conclusion: Explicit long-range interactions are necessary but not sufficient for predictive disordered silica modeling. Accurate MRO requires training data and sampling strategies that adequately represent the liquid-to-glass transition, not just architectural improvements like long-range attention mechanisms.

title: Decomposing Fractional Quantum Hall Wave Functions via Operator Contraction Multiplication
summary: We develop a general algebraic scheme to decompose fractional quantum Hall (FQH) wave functions based on the operator contraction multiplication. By introducing fermionic and bosonic operators and establishing three fundamental contraction rules, we achieve an exact decomposition of Laughlin states. This approach naturally extends to multi-component systems by factorizing coupled Jastrow factors via resultants and elementary symmetric polynomials, enabling the first complete decomposition of Halperin states. For Halperin ($2,2,1$) state, we explicitly derive its basic expansion, identify root configurations, and reveal intra- and inter-color squeezing operators, thereby uncovering the underlying generalized Pauli principle. Using this method, we compute orbital entanglement spectra for up to $16$ particles with decomposition dimensions exceeding $10^{11}$, obtaining edge excitation sequences that precisely match chiral Luttinger liquid theory. Our framework breaks through the longstanding limitations of Jack polynomials, provides a unified decomposition for both single- and multi-component FQH states, and opens a new avenue for exploring wave functions for more complex FQH states.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a general algebraic scheme to decompose fractional quantum Hall wave functions using operator contraction multiplication, enabling the first complete decomposition of multi-component Halperin states and overcoming limitations of Jack polynomial methods.
AI.motivation: The decomposition of fractional quantum Hall wave functions has been a challenging problem, with existing methods like Jack polynomials facing longstanding limitations, particularly for multi-component systems such as Halperin states.
AI.method: The authors introduce an algebraic scheme using operator contraction multiplication with fermionic and bosonic operators, establishing three fundamental contraction rules. For multi-component systems, they factorize coupled Jastrow factors via resultants and elementary symmetric polynomials.
AI.result: The method achieves exact decomposition of Laughlin states and the first complete decomposition of Halperin states. For the Halperin (2,2,1) state, the authors derive basic expansions, identify root configurations, reveal intra- and inter-color squeezing operators, and uncover the generalized Pauli principle. They compute orbital entanglement spectra for up to 16 particles with decomposition dimensions exceeding 10^11, obtaining edge excitation sequences that match chiral Luttinger liquid theory precisely.
AI.conclusion: This framework breaks through longstanding limitations of Jack polynomials and provides a unified decomposition method for both single- and multi-component FQH states. It opens new avenues for exploring wave functions for more complex FQH systems.

title: Velocity-field characteristics and device performance in nanoscale amorphous oxide Thin-Film-Transistors
summary: The electron velocity-electric field characteristics in short channel length (50-100 nm) amorphous oxide field-effect transistors (FETs) are described using measured experimental data from indium gallium zinc oxide (IGZO) FETs in conjunction with a physics-based model. Such understanding is crucial for the design of FETs for emerging applications such as in back-end-of-line circuitry for advanced memories and artificial intelligence hardware. In such semiconductor systems, there is an interplay between trapping and extended state (band) transport that has to be considered in detail for a more complete physical understanding of device operation. The approach described in this paper demonstrates such a method and its use for an exemplary semiconductor IGZO. It can be used in many emerging thin-film semiconductors, including several amorphous oxide semiconductors. The carrier mobility is calculated for dominant scattering mechanisms such as trapped carrier scattering and optical phonon scattering. The carrier velocity is computed from the mobility using a modified Caughey-Thomas equation. The physical model considers contact resistance, Joule heating, and electric-field-induced carrier heating, all of which are very important in small geometry FETs. The carrier velocity exhibits a tendency to saturate at high electric fields and reaches values > 2*10^6 cm/s when averaged over all induced carriers (both trapped and in the band) and > 4*10^6 cm/s for carriers in the band.
