title: Electrically switchable vacancy state revealed by in-operando positron experiments
summary: Whether the flash state in electrically driven solids involves non-equilibrium defect production or is accounted for by Joule heating alone has been debated since 2010. Using positron annihilation spectroscopy on copper, we observe a fully reversible, electrically switchable vacancy population: the DBS S-parameter rises above baseline whenever applied current exceeds a critical density and returns on current removal. Positron lifetime spectroscopy independently confirms open-volume defect formation and reveals a void to cluster relaxation hierarchy. The current-induced vacancy concentration exceeds the thermal-equilibrium value at 352C by > 106x, is present only while current is applied, and vanishes within minutes. The nucleation rate scales steeply with the applied current, connecting the minute-scale kinetics resolved here to the sub-second flash events observed in ceramic sintering. These results demonstrate current-induced Frenkel-pair production in a metal and identify a defect-mediated, non-equilibrium contribution to the flash state.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves a decade-long debate about the origin of the flash state in electrically driven solids by demonstrating that current-induced Frenkel-pair production (vacancy formation) in copper, rather than Joule heating alone, contributes to the flash phenomenon. Using positron annihilation spectroscopy, the authors observed a fully reversible, electrically switchable vacancy population that exceeds thermal equilibrium by over 10^6 times and exists only during current application.
AI.motivation: Since 2010, there has been debate about whether the flash state in electrically driven solids involves non-equilibrium defect production or is explained solely by Joule heating. This study aims to resolve this controversy by providing direct experimental evidence of current-induced defect formation.
AI.method: The researchers used two complementary positron annihilation spectroscopy techniques: (1) Doppler broadening spectroscopy (DBS) with S-parameter measurement to detect vacancy populations, and (2) positron lifetime spectroscopy to confirm open-volume defect formation and characterize defect types (voids vs. clusters). Experiments were conducted on copper specimens under various current densities.
AI.result: The study found: (1) A fully reversible, electrically switchable vacancy population - the DBS S-parameter rises above baseline when current exceeds a critical density and returns to baseline upon current removal; (2) Positron lifetime spectroscopy confirmed open-volume defect formation with a void-to-cluster relaxation hierarchy; (3) Current-induced vacancy concentration exceeds the thermal-equilibrium value at 352°C by more than 10^6 times; (4) Defects are present only during current application and vanish within minutes; (5) Nucleation rate scales steeply with applied current.
AI.conclusion: These results directly demonstrate current-induced Frenkel-pair production in a metal and identify a defect-mediated, non-equilibrium contribution to the flash state. The findings connect minute-scale kinetics observed here to sub-second flash events in ceramic sintering, suggesting a universal defect-mediated mechanism across materials systems.

title: Dynamical mean-field theory for dense spin systems at finite temperature
summary: In recent years, a method for computing spin dynamics at infinite temperature (spinDMFT) was developed. It utilizes the ideas of dynamical mean-field theory for fermions: single-site approximation and a self-consistency condition to approximate time-dependent spin correlations. In this work, we develop a crucial extension of the method to systems at finite temperature, able to compute imaginary-time correlations and thermodynamical quantities. We benchmark the method by comparison to results in finite-size systems, obtaining very good agreement with correlations in a random-coupling system, good agreement for a ferromagnetic system and large discrepancies in the case of an antiferromagnet. We note the appearance of ferromagnetic order in the method. We discuss possible extensions and potential applications of the approach.

AI.tldr: This work extends spin dynamical mean-field theory (spinDMFT) from infinite temperature to finite temperature calculations, enabling computation of imaginary-time correlations and thermodynamic quantities. The method is benchmarked against finite-size systems, showing very good agreement for random-coupling systems, good agreement for ferromagnets, but large discrepancies for antiferromagnets.
AI.motivation: The original spinDMFT method was developed only for infinite temperature spin dynamics. To compute thermodynamic quantities and imaginary-time correlations (relevant for finite-temperature physics), an extension to finite temperature was needed. This allows studying thermal properties and equilibrium spin correlations in many-body quantum systems.
AI.method: The extension applies the single-site approximation and self-consistency condition ideas from dynamical mean-field theory to finite temperature systems. Benchmarking was done by comparing results to exact diagonalization calculations in finite-size systems across three different cases: random-coupling, ferromagnetic, and antiferromagnetic systems.
AI.result: The method shows very good agreement with exact results for random-coupling systems and good agreement for ferromagnetic systems. However, large discrepancies appear for antiferromagnetic systems. Additionally, an unexpected appearance of ferromagnetic order was noted as an artifact of the approximations used.
AI.conclusion: The finite-temperature extension of spinDMFT shows promise for random-coupling and ferromagnetic systems but faces significant challenges with antiferromagnetic correlations. The ferromagnetic order artifact suggests the need for improved self-consistency conditions or extensions beyond the single-site approximation. The method could find applications in studying quantum magnets, thermalization in spin systems, and nonequilibrium dynamics at finite temperature.

title: Room-temperature third-order nonlinear anomalous Hall effect in ferromagnetic metal Fe3GaTe2
summary: Berry curvature, as the imaginary component of quantum geometry, plays a crucial role in condensed matter physics. The spatial distribution of Berry curvature can be characterized by its dipole and multipole moments, which can induce the nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (NLAHE). To date, the NLAHE has been demonstrated in various materials, yet reports on room-temperature NLAHE are still limited. In this work, we report the observation of the third-order NLAHE in ferromagnetic metal Fe3GaTe2. The third-order NLAHE shows hysteretic behavior with the variation of magnetic field, where the coercive field is the same as that of the anomalous Hall effect, and the third-order NLAHE remains observable up to the Curie temperature (~350 K). The scaling analysis suggests that the third-order NLAHE may be attributed to the Berry curvature quadrupole. Our work not only provides an approach to study magnetic materials through nonlinear electric transports, but also opens up possibilities for the future development of room-temperature third-order nonlinear electronic devices.

AI.tldr: This work reports the observation of third-order nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (NLAHE) in ferromagnetic metal Fe3GaTe2, demonstrating hysteretic behavior matching the coercive field of anomalous Hall effect, remaining observable up to ~350 K (Curie temperature). The scaling analysis suggests the effect originates from Berry curvature quadrupole.
AI.motivation: Room-temperature NLAHE has been limitedly reported despite the effect being demonstrated in various materials. This work addresses the need for room-temperature third-order nonlinear electronic devices.
AI.method: Transport measurements and scaling analysis were conducted on ferromagnetic metal Fe3GaTe2 to characterize the third-order NLAHE and its relationship with magnetic properties.
AI.result: Third-order NLAHE was observed in Fe3GaTe2 with hysteretic behavior matching the coercive field of anomalous Hall effect. The effect persists up to Curie temperature (~350 K) and may originate from Berry curvature quadrupole.
AI.conclusion: This work provides an approach to study magnetic materials through nonlinear electric transports and opens possibilities for room-temperature third-order nonlinear electronic devices.

title: Single-crystal growth and magnetic, magnetoelectric, and optical properties of ferroaxial-type SrMn$_2$Ni$_6$Te$_3$O$_{18}$
summary: Single crystals of SrMn$_2$Ni$_6$Te$_3$O$_{18}$, a member of the ferroaxial-type magnetic oxide family $AB_{2}C_{6}$Te$_3$O$_{18}$ ($A$ = Pb, Sr; $B$ = Mn, Cd; $C$ = Ni, Co), have been successfully grown, and their structural, magnetic, magnetoelectric, and optical properties have been systematically studied. Imaging of the spatial distribution of electric-field-induced optical rotation reveals that the single crystals preferentially form single ferroaxial (FA) domains. Magnetization and neutron diffraction measurements show that Mn$^{2+}$ and Ni$^{2+}$ magnetic moments order antiferromagnetically at $T_{\rm N}$ = 83 K, forming a $c$-axis collinear bidirector-type antiferromagnetic structure. All independent magnetoelectric tensor components allowed by the magnetic point group 6/$m^{\prime}$ have been detected, and the $χ_{33}$ component exhibits a pronounced temperature-dependent anomaly, including a peak and a sign reversal. Preferential formation of single FA domains and a similar $χ_{33}$ anomaly are also observed in the isostructural compound PbMn$_2$Ni$_6$Te$_3$O$_{18}$. These findings suggest that the ferroaxial and magnetic characteristics within this structural framework are robust against Sr-Pb replacement.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates that single crystals of ferroaxial magnetic oxides in the $AB_{2}C_{6}$Te$_3$O$_{18}$ family preferentially form single ferroaxial domains and exhibit antiferromagnetic ordering with robust magnetoelectric properties that persist across Sr-Pb substitution.
AI.motivation: Investigation of the ferroaxial-type magnetic oxide family $AB_{2}C_{6}$Te$_3$O$_{18}$ ($A$ = Pb, Sr; $B$ = Mn, Cd; $C$ = Ni, Co) and exploration of whether the ferroaxial and magnetic properties are robust against chemical substitution.
AI.method: Single crystal growth of SrMn$_2$Ni$_6$Te$_3$O$_{18}$ followed by comprehensive characterization using electric-field-induced optical rotation imaging, magnetization measurements, neutron diffraction, and magnetoelectric property studies, with comparison to the isostructural compound PbMn$_2$Ni$_6$Te$_3$O$_{18}$.
AI.result: Single crystals preferentially form single ferroaxial domains. Mn$^{2+}$ and Ni$^{2+}$ exhibit antiferromagnetic ordering at $T_{\rm N}$ = 83 K with a $c$-axis collinear bidirector-type structure. All magnetoelectric tensor components permitted by the 6/$m^{\prime}$ point group were detected, with $χ_{33}$ showing a temperature-dependent anomaly (peak and sign reversal). Similar ferroaxial domain preference and $χ_{33}$ anomaly were observed in PbMn$_2$Ni$_6$Te$_3$O$_{18}$.
AI.conclusion: The ferroaxial and magnetic characteristics within this structural framework are robust against Sr-Pb replacement, demonstrating that the fundamental physical properties are preserved across chemical substitution at the $A$-site.

title: Navigating Order-(Dis)Order Family Trees via Group-Subgroup Transitions
summary: As closed-loop materials discovery systems scale to produce millions of candidate compounds, the credibility of the novelty they reward becomes a critical concern. Novelty is commonly assessed against databases of ordered crystal structures, in which atomic sites are fully occupied. Yet, a predicted ordered structure may simply correspond to a particular ordering of a known disordered phase, whose sites are occupied by multiple species in the statistical average structure; we refer to such a structure as an ordered child of a disordered parent. Here, we introduce order-(dis)order family trees, a symmetry-based framework that organizes ordered and disordered structures through group-subgroup relations and enables novelty to be explicitly evaluated. We develop a high-throughput family matching procedure, to identify possible disordered parents and symmetry-related ordered relatives for a given ordered structure. As validation, we test our framework on synthesis-facing case studies (A-Lab), where it correctly recovers existing disordered parents for the targeted ordered structures. Extending this family-tree-based benchmark to experimental structure databases (ICSD), computational datasets (MP-20, Alex-MP-20, and GNoME), and crystal generative models further reveals that many ordered structures that appear novel as individual entries are, in fact, better understood as members of experimentally known order-(dis)order family trees. We also show that this is particularly evident in symmetry-agnostic all-atom generative models, which more frequently produce ordered structures derived from known disordered parents, whereas symmetry-constrained models are 2-4x less prone to this behavior. Our results establish order-(dis)order family trees as a key requirement for achieving genuine novelty in data-driven materials discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "order-(dis)order family trees" - a symmetry-based framework using group-subgroup relations to evaluate novelty in materials discovery. Many predicted "novel" ordered structures are actually ordered children of known disordered parent phases, revealing a critical credibility issue in materials screening.
AI.motivation: As closed-loop materials discovery systems produce millions of candidates, assessing novelty becomes critical. Current methods compare against ordered crystal structure databases, but miss that predicted ordered structures may simply be specific orderings of known disordered phases (where sites are occupied by multiple species statistically).
AI.method: Developed order-(dis)order family trees using group-subgroup relations to connect ordered and disordered structures. Created a high-throughput family matching procedure to identify possible disordered parents and symmetry-related ordered relatives for given ordered structures.
AI.result: Validation on A-Lab synthesis cases correctly recovered existing disordered parents. Extended testing on ICSD, MP-20, Alex-MP-20, and GNoME datasets revealed many "novel" ordered structures are actually members of known order-(dis)order families. Symmetry-agnostic generative models more frequently produce ordered structures from known disordered parents, while symmetry-constrained models are 2-4x less prone to this behavior.
AI.conclusion: Order-(dis)order family trees are essential for achieving genuine novelty in data-driven materials discovery. Current novelty assessments are incomplete without considering these family relationships.

title: Identifying Oriented Spin Space Groups and Related Physical Properties Using an Online Platform FINDSPINGROUP
summary: Unconventional magnets that combine antiferromagnetic structures with ferromagnetic-like responses are essential for the development of next-generation spintronics. Their emergent properties are fundamentally dictated by the interplay between exchange-driven magnetic geometry and spin-orbit coupling, which are described by spin space group (SSG) and magnetic space group (MSG) frameworks, respectively. However, the lack of direct correspondence between these frameworks, developed in different eras, hinders the systematic tracking of symmetry evolution of these intertwined physical contributions. In this work, we introduce FINDSPINGROUP, a computational architecture that implements the recently emerged, oriented spin space group framework to unify SSG and MSG descriptions. By automating the tracking of symmetry-breaking pathways from the non-relativistic to the relativistic limit, this online platform enables the classification of magnetic phases and the derivation of symmetry-constrained tensors for phenomena such as momentum-dependent spin splitting and the anomalous Hall effect. By standardizing data exchange through the spin crystallographic information file, this architecture establishes a computational infrastructure for the high-throughput discovery and design of unconventional magnets.

AI.tldr: FINDSPINGROUP is a computational platform that unifies spin space group (SSG) and magnetic space group (MSG) frameworks to automate symmetry analysis of unconventional magnets, enabling classification of magnetic phases and derivation of symmetry-constrained tensors for spintronic phenomena like momentum-dependent spin splitting and anomalous Hall effect.
AI.motivation: The development of next-generation spintronics requires unconventional magnets combining antiferromagnetic structures with ferromagnetic-like responses. However, the lack of direct correspondence between SSG and MSG frameworks (developed in different eras) hinders systematic tracking of symmetry evolution when exchange-driven magnetic geometry interacts with spin-orbit coupling. This gap prevents efficient discovery and design of these materials.
AI.method: The authors introduced FINDSPINGROUP, a computational architecture implementing the oriented spin space group framework to unify SSG and MSG descriptions. This online platform automates tracking of symmetry-breaking pathways from the non-relativistic to relativistic limit. It classifies magnetic phases and derives symmetry-constrained tensors while standardizing data exchange through the spin crystallographic information file.
AI.result: The platform enables classification of magnetic phases and derivation of symmetry-constrained tensors for phenomena including momentum-dependent spin splitting and anomalous Hall effect. It establishes a computational infrastructure for high-throughput discovery and design of unconventional magnets.
AI.conclusion: FINDSPINGROUP bridges the historical gap between SSG and MSG frameworks through the oriented spin space group approach, providing a unified computational tool for systematic symmetry analysis of magnetic materials. This work enables rational design of unconventional magnets for spintronic applications by standardizing data exchange and automating symmetry tracking across relativistic regimes.

title: GEWUM: General Exploration Workflow for the Utopia of Materials: A Unified Platform for Automated Structure Generation, Selection, and Validation
summary: The discovery of materials with tailored properties is increasingly reliant on computational methods. However, the fragmented landscape of existing software often hinders the seamless integration of large-scale structure prediction with rigorous stability validation, particularly in high-performance computing (HPC) environments. To address this gap, we present GEWUM (General Exploration Workflow for the Utopia of Materials), a unified, open-source platform designed to automate and accelerate materials discovery. GEWUM integrates the Selective Random Structure Search (SRSS) strategy with universal Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (uMLIPs), enabling efficient exploration of vast chemical spaces. Its core architecture features a modular design with native support for SLURM-based HPC clusters. The platform unifies the entire workflow, from random structure generation and diversity-preserving selection to thermodynamic and dynamic stability assessments, as well as advanced property calculations (e.g., elastic constants, thermal conductivity, and quasi-harmonic approximations). We demonstrate GEWUM's capabilities through three distinct case studies: (1) the prediction of low-energy polymorphs in the complex Al-Sc-N nitride system; (2) the identification of a P-62c phase of U3Si5, distinct from the known AlB2 type; and (3) the high-pressure structure prediction of ThH10 at 150 GPa. Furthermore, benchmark tests show reasonable agreement in predicting thermophysical properties. By bridging the gap between uMLIPs and automated high-throughput workflows, GEWUM serves as a valuable framework to facilitate efficient and scalable materials exploration.

AI.tldr: GEWUM is an open-source, automated platform for high-throughput materials discovery that integrates the SRSS (Selective Random Structure Search) strategy with uMLIPs (universal Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials). It is designed for HPC environments (SLURM-based clusters) and unifies the entire workflow from structure generation to stability assessment and property calculations.
AI.motivation: The fragmented landscape of existing computational materials software hinders seamless integration of large-scale structure prediction with rigorous stability validation, particularly in HPC environments. There was a need for a unified platform that automates and accelerates materials discovery.
AI.method: GEWUM uses a modular architecture with native SLURM support for HPC clusters. It integrates SRSS strategy with uMLIPs for efficient exploration of chemical spaces. The platform performs: (1) random structure generation, (2) diversity-preserving selection, (3) thermodynamic and dynamic stability assessments, and (4) advanced property calculations (elastic constants, thermal conductivity, quasi-harmonic approximations).
AI.result: Validated through three case studies: (1) prediction of low-energy polymorphs in Al-Sc-N nitride system, (2) identification of P-62c phase of U3Si5 (distinct from AlB2 type), and (3) high-pressure structure prediction of ThH10 at 150 GPa. Benchmark tests show reasonable agreement in predicting thermophysical properties.
AI.conclusion: GEWUM successfully bridges the gap between uMLIPs and automated high-throughput workflows, serving as a valuable framework for efficient and scalable materials exploration. It represents a significant advancement in computational materials discovery by providing a unified, open-source platform.

title: Data-Driven Thermal and Mechanical Modeling of Defective Covalent Organic Frameworks
summary: Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) are versatile two-dimensional (2D) materials for flexible electronics, catalysis, and sensing, owing to their tunable architectures and large surface areas. However, like most materials, COFs inevitably contain synthesis-induced defects, which-similar to graphene-can strongly influence intrinsic properties, such as thermal transport and mechanical strength. To address this challenge, we have assessed the performance of a set of machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIP) capable of efficient large-scale simulations of COFs with quantum accuracy. In doing so, QCOF models (Quantum COF) were developed by tuning the state-of-the-art MACE architecture on an extensive dataset of non-equilibrium COF conformations generated from high-fidelity density functional theory calculations. The accuracy, computational efficiency, memory footprint, and transferability to unseen chemical environments of these models were benchmarked against general-purpose MACE models and their fine-tuned variants. Our results show that an invariant QCOF model with a small descriptor dimensionality and cutoff outperforms all other models in most validation tasks, including scalability to large systems, force prediction in defective COFs, and phonon dispersion calculations. The best-performing QCOF model was then used to run large-scale simulations of thermal conductivity for defective CTF-1 and COF-LZU1 systems via non-equilibrium MD, revealing a more pronounced sensitivity of CTF-1 to structural defects. Stress-strain curves were also investigated, showing that the mechanical response remains nearly invariant at low defect densities, while asymmetric behaviour emerges at large strains. This work thus provides a foundation for the design of robust quantum-informed MLIP for large-scale property simulations of defective of extended network materials.

AI.tldr: This paper develops quantum-informed machine learning interatomic potentials (QCOF) for large-scale simulations of defective covalent organic frameworks (COFs), demonstrating superior performance in predicting thermal transport and mechanical properties compared to general-purpose models.
AI.motivation: COFs are promising 2D materials for electronics, catalysis, and sensing, but synthesis-induced defects significantly impact their intrinsic properties (thermal transport, mechanical strength). Existing methods lacked efficient large-scale simulation capabilities with quantum accuracy for these defective systems.
AI.method: The researchers developed QCOF models by fine-tuning the MACE architecture on an extensive dataset of non-equilibrium COF conformations from DFT calculations. They benchmarked accuracy, computational efficiency, memory footprint, and transferability against general-purpose MACE models. The best model was applied to large-scale NEMD simulations of thermal conductivity for CTF-1 and COF-LZU1 systems, plus stress-strain curve calculations.
AI.result: The invariant QCOF model with small descriptor dimensionality and cutoff outperformed all others in scalability, force prediction in defective COFs, and phonon dispersion calculations. NEMD simulations revealed CTF-1 shows more pronounced sensitivity to structural defects than COF-LZU1. Mechanical response remained nearly invariant at low defect densities, but asymmetric behavior emerged at large strains.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundation for designing robust quantum-informed MLIP for large-scale property simulations of defective extended network materials, enabling efficient prediction of thermal and mechanical properties in COF systems.

title: Emergence of a non-bulk hexagonal Fe$_2$S$_2$ single layer via phase transformation
summary: Two-dimensional materials can stabilize crystal structures that are absent from their bulk counterparts, offering opportunities for materials design. Here, we report the synthesis of a previously unknown hexagonal Fe$_2$S$_2$ single layer with $β$-CuI structure, a buckled layer of two vertically stacked FeS honeycomb lattices, realized by thermally induced transformation of single layer mackinawite grown on graphene/Ir(111). In situ scanning tunneling microscopy and low-energy electron diffraction reveal a transition from a tetragonal to a hexagonal lattice accompanied by distinct morphological and electronic signatures. The hexagonal Fe$_2$S$_2$ forms reproducibly upon annealing and represents a new structural motif within the Fe-S material family. First-principles calculations identify the $β$-CuI structure as most consistent with experiment. The calculations suggest that on-site Coulomb interactions and magnetic order are relevant to understanding the stability of the new 2D Fe-S compound. The preferred nucleation of single-layer mackinawite, despite being energetically disfavored, is speculated to result from its low edge energy, analogous to the 3D case. Our results establish Fe$_2$S$_2$ as a platform for exploring structural polymorphism in two dimensions and demonstrate that reduced dimensionality can stabilize crystal structures not accessible in bulk materials.

AI.tldr: Researchers synthesized a new 2D hexagonal Fe₂S₂ layer with β-CuI structure by thermally transforming single-layer mackinawite on graphene/Ir(111). STM and LEED revealed a tetragonal-to-hexagonal transition. First-principles calculations confirmed the structure and showed magnetic interactions affect stability. This demonstrates 2D materials can access crystal structures unavailable in bulk.
AI.motivation: Two-dimensional materials can stabilize crystal structures absent from bulk counterparts, offering opportunities for materials design. This study aims to discover new 2D crystal structures by exploring the Fe-S system.
AI.method: Single-layer mackinawite was grown on graphene/Ir(111) and thermally transformed to create a hexagonal Fe₂S₂ layer. The transformation was studied using in situ scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and low-energy electron diffraction (LEED). First-principles calculations were performed to identify the most likely atomic structure.
AI.result: A previously unknown hexagonal Fe₂S₂ single layer with β-CuI structure (buckled layer of two vertically stacked FeS honeycomb lattices) was synthesized. A transition from tetragonal to hexagonal lattice was observed with distinct morphological and electronic signatures. First-principles calculations confirmed the β-CuI structure and indicated that on-site Coulomb interactions and magnetic order are relevant to stability. The preferred nucleation of single-layer mackinawite despite being energetically disfavored is attributed to low edge energy.
AI.conclusion: The hexagonal Fe₂S₂ with β-CuI structure represents a new structural motif within the Fe-S material family. This work demonstrates that reduced dimensionality can stabilize crystal structures not accessible in bulk materials and establishes Fe₂S₂ as a platform for exploring structural polymorphism in two dimensions.

title: Self-consistent evaluation of the Berry connection for Wannier functions
summary: The Berry connection is a gauge-dependent quantity frequently used to describe the optical response of solids. Its evaluation requires a k-derivative with respect to the cell periodic-part of the Bloch-functions and is commonly calculated in the Wannier basis by using overlap matrices of cell-periodic parts of Bloch-functions at neighboring k-points. So far, all proposed interpolation schemes for the Berry connection do not account for the matrix structure of the overlap matrices explicitly but treat the matrix elements as independent, or only distinguish between diagonal and off-diagonal entries. In this work, we propose a self-consistent interpolation scheme based on the matrix logarithm resulting in a strongly improved accuracy. Furthermore, we discuss how the basis set incompleteness of the bands used in the ab-initio calculation imposes constraints on the accuracy. We quantify the basis incompleteness based on the singular values of the overlap matrices and relate it to the invariant part of the spread functional $Ω_\mathrm{I}$ of the Wannier functions. Numerical calculations for monolayer MoS2 and bulk Si demonstrate that the proposed interpolation scheme is much less sensitive to the Wannierization details and leads to an improved quality of the velocity matrix and the optical conductivity.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a self-consistent interpolation scheme for the Berry connection based on matrix logarithm, which improves accuracy over existing methods that treat matrix elements as independent. The method is demonstrated on monolayer MoS2 and bulk Si, showing reduced sensitivity to Wannierization details and improved optical conductivity calculations.
AI.motivation: The Berry connection is gauge-dependent and requires k-derivatives of cell-periodic Bloch functions. Current interpolation schemes don't properly account for the matrix structure of overlap matrices, treating elements as independent or only distinguishing diagonal/off-diagonal entries. This limits accuracy in calculating optical responses of solids.
AI.method: The authors propose a self-consistent interpolation scheme based on matrix logarithm. They quantify basis set incompleteness using singular values of overlap matrices and relate it to the invariant part of the spread functional Ω_I of Wannier functions. The method is applied to monolayer MoS2 and bulk Si for validation.
AI.result: The proposed interpolation scheme is much less sensitive to Wannierization details and leads to improved quality of velocity matrix and optical conductivity calculations compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The matrix logarithm-based approach provides a more accurate and robust method for Berry connection interpolation, addressing limitations of prior approaches and providing better results for optical property calculations in solids.

title: Nickel intercalation in epitaxial graphene on SiC(0001): a novel platform for engineering two-dimensional heterostructures
summary: Two-dimensional (2D) magnetic materials integrated with graphene offer a compelling platform for next-generation spintronic devices, yet nickel in its 2D form remains largely unexplored, due to fundamental synthesis limitations. Here, we report the controlled intercalation of Ni beneath epitaxial graphene on the Si-face of SiC(0001), achieved through a scalable colloidal nanoparticle deposition route. Chemically synthesized Ni nanoparticles (~10 nm diameter) are uniformly deposited onto graphene via immersion in colloidal solution at room temperature; subsequent thermal annealing at 650 °C drives intercalation, yielding well-ordered Ni islands at the graphene/buffer-layer interface with morphology dictated by annealing conditions. Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), supported by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, elucidate the atomic and electronic structure of the intercalated layers. DFT simulations further confirm the thermodynamic stability of the 2D nanostructures as a function of shape and lateral size, predicting a robust average magnetic moment of 0.9 $μ_B$ per atom. The resulting Ni-intercalated graphene on SiC constitutes a well-defined 2D heterostructure combining preserved graphene band structure with robust interfacial magnetism, stable under ambient conditions. These findings establish a reproducible, scalable pathway to engineer magnetic graphene-based heterostructures and open new avenues for their integration into spintronic architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper reports a scalable colloidal nanoparticle deposition method to intercalate Ni beneath epitaxial graphene on SiC(0001), creating stable 2D Ni islands with a magnetic moment of 0.9 μ_B per atom, opening pathways for spintronic applications.
AI.motivation: Two-dimensional magnetic materials integrated with graphene are promising for next-generation spintronic devices, but 2D Ni has been largely unexplored due to fundamental synthesis limitations.
AI.method: The researchers used a colloidal nanoparticle deposition route: depositing ~10 nm Ni nanoparticles onto graphene via immersion in colloidal solution at room temperature, followed by thermal annealing at 650°C to drive intercalation. The resulting structures were characterized using STM, ARPES, and DFT calculations.
AI.result: The process yielded well-ordered Ni islands at the graphene/buffer-layer interface with morphology controlled by annealing conditions. DFT confirmed thermodynamic stability of the 2D nanostructures and predicted a robust average magnetic moment of 0.9 μ_B per atom. The intercalated structure preserves graphene's band structure while introducing robust interfacial magnetism, stable under ambient conditions.
AI.conclusion: This study establishes a reproducible, scalable pathway to engineer magnetic graphene-based heterostructures, enabling their integration into spintronic architectures.

title: Neural surrogates for crystal growth dynamics with variable supersaturation: explicit vs. implicit conditioning
summary: Simulations of crystal growth are performed by using Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network surrogate models, trained on a dataset of time sequences computed by numerical integration of Allen-Cahn dynamics including faceting via kinetic anisotropy. Two network architectures are developed to take into account the effects of a variable supersaturation value. The first infers it implicitly by processing an input mini-sequence of a few evolution frames and then returns a consistent continuation of the evolution. The second takes the supersaturation parameter as an explicit input along with a single initial frame and predicts the entire sequence. The two models are systematically tested to establish strengths and weaknesses, comparing the prediction performance for models trained on datasets of different size and, in the first architecture, different lengths of input mini-sequence. The analysis of point-wise and mean absolute errors shows how the explicit parameter conditioning guarantees the best results, reproducing with high-fidelity the ground-truth profiles. Comparable results are achievable by the mini-sequence approach only when using larger training datasets. The trained models show strong conditioning by the supersaturation parameter, consistently reproducing its overall impact on growth rates as well as its local effect on the faceted morphology. Moreover, they are perfectly scalable even on 256 times larger domains and can be successfully extended to more than 10 times longer sequences with limited error accumulation. The analysis highlights the potential and limits of these approaches in view of their general exploitation for crystal growth simulations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops CNN-RNN surrogate models for accelerating crystal growth simulations by learning Allen-Cahn dynamics with faceting. Two architectures are compared: one using implicit supersaturation inference from mini-sequences, and another using explicit supersaturation as input. The explicit conditioning approach performs best, with both models showing strong scalability and capability to reproduce supersaturation effects on growth rates and morphology.
AI.motivation: Crystal growth simulations via numerical integration are computationally expensive. There is a need for surrogate models that can accelerate simulations while maintaining accuracy in predicting crystal morphology evolution under varying supersaturation conditions.
AI.method: Two CNN-RNN architectures were developed: 1) Implicit model that processes input mini-sequences of evolution frames to predict continuations, and 2) Explicit model that takes supersaturation parameter as input along with single initial frame to predict entire sequences. Models were trained on numerical integration data of Allen-Cahn dynamics with faceting via kinetic anisotropy. Systematic testing involved comparing prediction performance across different training dataset sizes and input mini-sequence lengths.
AI.result: Explicit parameter conditioning yields the best performance with high-fidelity reproduction of ground-truth profiles. Comparable results with mini-sequence approach require larger training datasets. Both approaches show strong supersaturation parameter conditioning, reproducing growth rates and faceted morphology effects. Models scale to 256x larger domains and extend to 10x longer sequences with limited error accumulation.
AI.conclusion: The explicit parameter conditioning approach achieves the best results, reproducing ground-truth profiles with high fidelity. The mini-sequence approach requires larger training datasets to achieve comparable results. Both models show strong conditioning by supersaturation parameters, reproducing their impact on growth rates and faceted morphology. The models are scalable to 256x larger domains and extendable to 10x longer sequences with limited error accumulation. The study highlights both the potential and limitations of these neural network approaches for crystal growth simulations.

title: Amorphous Nanoconfinement Enables Self-sustaining Sabatier Reaction at Ambient Conditions
summary: The Sabatier reaction, the catalytic hydrogenation of CO2 into CH4, offers a cornerstone for carbon capture and utilization, and in-situ resource utilization during space exploration; however, it faces a fundamental thermodynamic-kinetic paradox: although highly exothermic, conventional catalysts still require continuous external heating to activate CO2 and maintain stable operation. Here we report an amorphous silica-embedded ruthenium catalyst that enables a long-term self-sustaining autothermal Sabatier reaction dispensing with external energy supply. Operating under ambient conditions, this system achieves a record-high CH4 yield of 0.50 mol gcat-1 h-1 with 100% selectivity, stable operation for over 2,000 hours, and a record-low catalyst bed temperature down to 100 oC. This exceptional self-sustaining behavior stems from the synergistic effect of the catalyst's ultralow effective thermal conductivity (0.27 W m-1 K-1), induced by amorphous nanoconfinement, and its superior intrinsic activity. This synergy generates localized hot spots at Ru sites while suppressing macroscopic heat loss. In situ measurements further reveal CH4 formation even at 54 oC and identify a *CO-mediated pathway for CO2 methanation. The reaction ignites readily with a lighter or focused sunlight and persists even under forced convection from an electric fan, demonstrating strong environmental tolerance. By removing the need for constant energy input, this "ignite-and-forget" system paves the way for decentralized Power-to-Gas systems and autonomous fuel production in resource-constrained environments like Mars.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed an amorphous silica-embedded Ru catalyst that enables self-sustaining CO2 methanation without external heating by leveraging ultra-low thermal conductivity (0.27 W m-1 K-1) to create localized hot spots. This "ignite-and-forget" system achieves record performance metrics—0.50 mol/g/h CH4 yield, 100% selectivity, 2,000+ hour stability, and operation at 100°C—paving the way for decentralized Power-to-Gas and autonomous space exploration fuel production.
AI.motivation: The Sabatier reaction (CO2 + 4H2 → CH4 + 2H2O) is critically important for carbon capture and utilization (CCU) and space exploration (in-situ resource utilization/ISRU). However, a fundamental paradox exists: despite being highly exothermic, conventional catalysts require continuous external heating due to high activation barriers and heat loss. This limitation hinders decentralized and autonomous applications where continuous energy input is unavailable.
AI.method: The researchers developed an amorphous silica-embedded ruthenium (Ru) catalyst utilizing nanoconfinement effects to achieve ultra-low effective thermal conductivity (0.27 W m-1 K-1). This property creates a unique thermal management strategy where localized hot spots form at Ru active sites while macroscopic heat loss is suppressed. In situ spectroscopic measurements were employed to elucidate the reaction mechanism, identifying a *CO-mediated pathway for CO2 methanation.
AI.result: The catalyst achieved a record-high CH4 yield of 0.50 mol gcat-1 h-1 with 100% selectivity. Stable operation was maintained for over 2,000 hours. The system achieved the record-low catalyst bed temperature of 100°C. In situ measurements confirmed CH4 formation at temperatures as low as 54°C. The reaction demonstrates strong environmental tolerance, igniting easily with a lighter or concentrated sunlight and persisting under forced convection from an electric fan.
AI.conclusion: This work successfully demonstrates a groundbreaking "ignite-and-forget" Sabatier system that achieves record-low temperature operation (100°C), exceptional long-term stability (2,000+ hours), and complete CH4 selectivity. The innovation lies in an amorphous silica-embedded Ru catalyst where ultra-low thermal conductivity creates localized hot spots, enabling autothermal operation without external energy input. This system has significant implications for decentralized Power-to-Gas applications and autonomous fuel production in space exploration, effectively resolving the traditional thermodynamic-kinetic paradox of CO2 methanation.

title: Nearly Complete Charge--Spin Conversion via Strain-Eliminated Fermi Pockets in a $d$-Wave Altermagnet
summary: The room-temperature altermagnet \mathrm{KV_2Se_2O} possesses nearly orthogonal flat Fermi surfaces, which in an idealized $d$-wave limit enable complete spin-channel separation and a theoretical charge-to-spin conversion efficiency (CSE) of 100%. Realistic samples, however, host residual elliptical Fermi pockets that enhance charge conductivity while suppressing spin conductivity, drastically reducing the CSE. Here we show that in-plane equibiaxial tensile strain systematically eliminates these parasitic pockets, restoring the flat-band geometry. Our first-principles calculations reveal that the CSE increases monotonically with strain, reaching a record value of approximately 96% at 4% strain. An effective tight-binding model fitted to the computed band structure accurately captures the evolution of the Fermi surface and confirms that the suppression of the pockets -- governed by reduced next-nearest-neighbor hoppings -- is the dominant mechanism for the strain-enhanced CSE. We further identify an unconventional out-of-plane spin current that emerges under tilted electric fields and achieves a CSE of nearly 55% at optimal orientations, offering a promising pathway for field-free perpendicular magnetization switching. Our work establishes strain engineering as a practical route to approach the ultimate conversion limit in altermagnets and provides a design principle for high-efficiency spintronic devices.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that in-plane equibiaxial tensile strain can enhance the charge-to-spin conversion efficiency (CSE) in altermagnet KV2Se2O from low values to approximately 96% at 4% strain by eliminating parasitic elliptical Fermi pockets, with an additional out-of-plane spin current achieving ~55% CSE under tilted electric fields.
AI.motivation: Realistic KV2Se2O samples have residual elliptical Fermi pockets that enhance charge conductivity while suppressing spin conductivity, drastically reducing the CSE from the theoretical 100% in the ideal d-wave limit.
AI.method: First-principles calculations combined with an effective tight-binding model fitted to the computed band structure.
AI.result: The CSE increases monotonically with strain, reaching a record value of approximately 96% at 4% strain. An unconventional out-of-plane spin current under tilted electric fields achieves a CSE of nearly 55% at optimal orientations.
AI.conclusion: Strain engineering is a practical route to approach the ultimate conversion limit in altermagnets, providing a design principle for high-efficiency spintronic devices and field-free perpendicular magnetization switching.

title: Direct observation of surface bandgap shrinkage and negative electronic compressibility in SrTiO3
summary: In this work, we investigate and compare the electronic structures of SrTiO3 and KTaO3 under ultraviolet (UV) light induced electron doping. Using angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), the evolution of the surface electronic structures of SrTiO3 and KTaO3 is systematically examined as a function of electron density. In contrast to KTaO3, SrTiO3 exhibits a pronounced shrinking of its surface bandgap by approximately 390 meV, accompanied by a counterintuitive shift of the valence band peak toward lower binding energies of up to 200 meV with increasing electron density. This anomalous behavior constitutes a spectroscopic signature of negative electronic compressibility (NEC). Density-functional-theory calculations provide qualitative support for the experimental observations. The calculations show that surface formation already reduces the apparent near-gap separation in SrTiO3, while additional electron accumulation further drives the slab toward a more metallic state; oxygen-vacancy models likewise produce strong bandgap reduction, identifying plausible mechanisms contributing to the observed surface bandgap shrinkage. These findings establish a direct spectroscopic link between bandgap engineering and the NEC effect at the SrTiO3 surface, highlighting the potential of SrTiO3 for next-generation oxide electronic, optoelectronic, and high-performance capacitive energy storage devices applications.

AI.tldr: Study investigates electronic structures of SrTiO3 and KTaO3 under UV light-induced electron doping using ARPES and DFT. SrTiO3 shows anomalous bandgap shrinkage (~390 meV) and valence band shift toward lower binding energies, signature of negative electronic compressibility (NEC), while KTaO3 does not exhibit this behavior.
AI.motivation: Understanding and comparing the electronic structure evolution of perovskite oxides SrTiO3 and KTaO3 under electron doping, particularly to investigate the negative electronic compressibility (NEC) effect and its potential for bandgap engineering in oxide-based devices.
AI.method: Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) was used to systematically examine surface electronic structure evolution as a function of electron density. Density-functional-theory (DFT) calculations were performed to provide qualitative support, including oxygen-vacancy models.
AI.result: SrTiO3 exhibits pronounced bandgap shrinkage of ~390 meV and counterintuitive valence band peak shift toward lower binding energies (up to 200 meV) with increasing electron density—characteristic of NEC. KTaO3 shows no such behavior. DFT calculations confirm surface formation reduces near-gap separation, and electron accumulation drives the slab toward metallic state; oxygen-vacancy models produce strong bandgap reduction.
AI.conclusion: Findings establish direct spectroscopic link between bandgap engineering and NEC effect at SrTiO3 surface, demonstrating SrTiO3's potential for next-generation oxide electronic, optoelectronic, and high-performance capacitive energy storage devices.

title: Electronic and Vibrational Properties of On-Surface Synthesized Gulf-Edged Chiral Graphene Nanoribbons
summary: On-surface synthesis enables the fabrication of graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) with atomic precision, allowing their electronic, optical, and magnetic properties to be tuned by engineering edge structure and width. Progress on the synthesis of chiral GNRs has nevertheless remained limited, largely because existing precursor designs rely on laterally fused acene units and cannot access edge topologies beyond armchair and zigzag. Here, we introduce a new on-surface synthesis motif that yields a gulf-edged chiral GNR. The growth steps are monitored by scanning probe microscopy, and the atomic structure is confirmed by non-contact atomic force microscopy. Scanning tunneling spectroscopy combined with theoretical simulations identifies the gulf-edged chiral GNR as a closed-shell semiconductor with a bandgap of 1.8 eV. Raman spectroscopy reveals vibrational properties, including a distinctive mode that may serve as a fingerprint for chiral GNRs. The Raman analysis further uncovers ambient instability despite the large bandgap and non-spin-polarized edges, consistent with prior reports linking GNR stability to zigzag edge features. This work establishes a rationally designed synthesis motif for chiral GNRs and provides a combined structural, electronic, and vibrational characterization, offering guidelines for future synthesis strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new on-surface synthesis motif for fabricating gulf-edged chiral graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), achieving a previously inaccessible edge topology. The chiral GNR is characterized as a closed-shell semiconductor with a 1.8 eV bandgap, and exhibits a distinctive Raman mode that could serve as a fingerprint. Despite the large bandgap, ambient instability was observed, suggesting GNR stability depends on factors beyond bandgap and edge spin polarization.
AI.motivation: Previous synthesis approaches for graphene nanoribbons relied on laterally fused acene units, which could only access armchair and zigzag edge topologies. Chiral GNRs remained inaccessible, limiting the ability to tune electronic, optical, and magnetic properties through edge structure engineering.
AI.method: On-surface synthesis monitored by scanning probe microscopy, with atomic structure confirmed by non-contact atomic force microscopy (NC-AFM). Electronic properties characterized by scanning tunneling spectroscopy combined with theoretical simulations. Vibrational properties investigated by Raman spectroscopy.
AI.result: Successfully synthesized a gulf-edged chiral GNR - a previously inaccessible edge topology. Characterized as a closed-shell semiconductor with a bandgap of 1.8 eV. Raman spectroscopy revealed a distinctive vibrational mode that may serve as a fingerprint for chiral GNRs. However, the study also uncovered ambient instability despite the large bandgap and non-spin-polarized edges, which is consistent with prior reports linking GNR stability to zigzag edge features.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a rationally designed synthesis motif for chiral GNRs and provides comprehensive structural, electronic, and vibrational characterization. The successful synthesis of gulf-edged chiral GNR (a previously inaccessible edge topology) offers guidelines for future synthesis strategies. The finding that ambient instability persists despite large bandgap and non-spin-polarized edges suggests that stability in GNRs may be more complex than previously thought.

title: Optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect reveals the hidden spin order in antiferromagnets
comment: v1: preprint; licence: CC BY 4.0, supplementary material is a part of this submission
summary: Reading antiferromagnetic order remains a central obstacle for antiferromagnetic memory and logic because zero net magnetisation precludes conventional magnetic readout. Domain imaging typically relies on x-ray magnetic linear dichroism (XMLD) microscopy at synchrotron sources, but XMLD is even under time reversal and cannot distinguish 180°-reversed magnetic states. Here we report the first experimental observation of the optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect, predicted for antiferromagnets with combined parity - time-reversal ($PT$) symmetry. The effect stems from light-induced interband electric-dipole transitions, where spin-orbit coupling induces an asymmetry between $\pm k$ states and generates a time-reversal-odd photocurrent whose sign flips upon 180° reversal of the Néel vector. In $PT$-symmetric CuMnAs, we use near-field excitation to map this photocurrent with sub-100-nm spatial resolution after current-induced spin-orbit-torque switching. The signal polarity follows local Néel vector reversal, enabling nanoscale imaging of antiferromagnetic texture and direct readout of 180°-reversed antiferromagnetic states that remain indistinguishable in XMLD and other time-reversal-even linear-dichroic probes. The optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect thus reveals a new light-spin interaction and provides a scalable route to nanoscale readout of hidden spin order, with potential for ultrafast all-electrical and all-optical antiferromagnetic spintronic technologies.

AI.tldr: Researchers demonstrate the first optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect in antiferromagnets, enabling nanoscale imaging and readout of 180°-reversed antiferromagnetic states that cannot be distinguished by conventional time-reversal-even techniques like XMLD. This new light-spin interaction mechanism opens pathways for ultrafast antiferromagnetic spintronic technologies.
AI.motivation: Reading antiferromagnetic order is challenging due to zero net magnetization, which prevents conventional magnetic readout. Existing domain imaging techniques like X-ray magnetic linear dichroism (XMLD) are time-reversal-even and cannot distinguish 180°-reversed magnetic states, creating a fundamental obstacle for antiferromagnetic memory and logic applications.
AI.method: The study employs light-induced interband electric-dipole transitions in PT-symmetric CuMnAs, where spin-orbit coupling generates asymmetry between ±k states, producing a time-reversal-odd photocurrent. Near-field excitation enables mapping this photocurrent with sub-100-nm spatial resolution after spin-orbit-torque switching.
AI.result: First experimental observation of the optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect in PT-symmetric CuMnAs. The generated photocurrent's sign flips upon 180° reversal of the Néel vector, enabling sub-100-nm spatial resolution mapping of antiferromagnetic texture and direct readout of 180°-reversed states that remain indistinguishable in XMLD and other time-reversal-even linear dichroic probes.
AI.conclusion: The optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect reveals a new light-spin interaction mechanism and provides a scalable route to nanoscale readout of hidden spin order in antiferromagnets. This discovery holds significant potential for developing ultrafast all-electrical and all-optical antiferromagnetic spintronic technologies.

title: Modeling High Entropy Alloys' Mechanical Property through Natural Language-Derived Descriptors
summary: Processing treatments of alloys, despite being influential to alloy properties, are often neglected in machine-learning aided alloy designs due to the difficulties in expressing this information. We investigated the expressiveness of transformer embeddings through synthesized annealing processing treatment text and verified that embeddings could be utilized to reconstruct the processing parameters of alloys effectively with an R2>0.99. We then utilized the vector representations of alloys' processing treatment descriptions as descriptors to model high-entropy alloys' hardness and achieved a 20% improvement in prediction, verifying that natural language-derived descriptors of processing treatment information could be utilized to improve prediction of alloy properties.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel approach to incorporate processing treatment information into machine learning models for alloy property prediction by using transformer embeddings derived from natural language descriptions of annealing treatments. The method achieves over 0.99 R² in reconstructing processing parameters and improves hardness prediction by 20% for high-entropy alloys.
AI.motivation: Processing treatments significantly influence alloy properties but are often overlooked in ML-aided alloy design due to the difficulty in representing this information numerically. This work addresses this gap by leveraging transformer embeddings to encode processing treatment text.
AI.method: The researchers used transformer embeddings to encode synthesized annealing processing treatment text, then tested whether these embeddings could reconstruct alloy processing parameters. They subsequently used the vector representations as descriptors to model high-entropy alloy hardness.
AI.result: The embeddings achieved R² > 0.99 in reconstructing processing parameters, demonstrating that transformer embeddings can effectively capture processing treatment information. Using these natural language-derived descriptors improved hardness prediction by 20%.
AI.conclusion: Natural language-derived descriptors of processing treatment information can successfully improve alloy property predictions. This approach bridges the gap between qualitative processing knowledge and quantitative ML models, offering a new pathway for incorporating treatment history into alloy design pipelines.

title: Critical role of phase-dependent properties in modeling photothermal sintering of LiCoO2 cathodes
summary: Photothermal (photonic) sintering crystallizes as-deposited amorphous LiCoO2 (LCO) cathodes for solid-state thin-film batteries using millisecond, surface-localized heating. However, process design often relies on 1D models with phase-averaged, temperature-independent properties, which can mispredict peak temperatures and thermal damage margins. Here we develop a multiscale, data-driven framework that provides phase- and grain size-resolved thermophysical inputs for stoichiometric LCO. We train an Allegro neural network potential with near-ab initio accuracy, enabling Green-Kubo calculations of thermal conductivity for crystalline and amorphous phases. The low, weakly density-dependent conductivity of amorphous LCO motivates its use as an effective intergranular phase in a thin-interface model that reproduces observed grain-size-dependent thermal transport. Combined with measured wavelength-resolved optical properties in 1D multiphysics simulations, we show amorphous LCO absorbs more strongly and reaches higher peak temperatures than crystalline LCO; thus crystalline, constant-property models systematically overestimate safe operating windows.

AI.tldr: A multiscale, data-driven framework was developed to model photothermal sintering of LiCoO2 cathodes for solid-state thin-film batteries. Using neural network potentials and Green-Kubo calculations, the study found that amorphous LCO has low, weakly density-dependent thermal conductivity and absorbs more strongly than crystalline LCO, reaching higher peak temperatures. This reveals that conventional models using constant properties systematically overestimate safe operating windows.
AI.motivation: Photothermal sintering is used to crystallize as-deposited amorphous LiCoO2 cathodes for solid-state thin-film batteries using millisecond, surface-localized heating. However, existing process design relies on simplified 1D models with phase-averaged, temperature-independent properties, which can mispredict peak temperatures and thermal damage margins. This limitation motivated the development of a more accurate modeling framework.
AI.method: The researchers developed a multiscale, data-driven framework that provides phase- and grain size-resolved thermophysical inputs for stoichiometric LCO. They trained an Allegro neural network potential with near-ab initio accuracy to enable Green-Kubo calculations of thermal conductivity for both crystalline and amorphous phases. This was combined with measured wavelength-resolved optical properties in 1D multiphysics simulations. The framework uses a thin-interface model incorporating amorphous LCO as an effective intergranular phase to reproduce observed grain-size-dependent thermal transport.
AI.result: The study found that amorphous LCO exhibits low, weakly density-dependent thermal conductivity. The multiscale framework successfully reproduces observed grain-size-dependent thermal transport. Importantly, the simulations show that amorphous LCO absorbs more strongly and reaches higher peak temperatures than crystalline LCO under photothermal sintering conditions. This finding challenges the conventional approach of using crystalline, constant-property models.
AI.conclusion: This study reveals that amorphous LCO absorbs more strongly and reaches higher peak temperatures than crystalline LCO under photothermal sintering conditions. The key insight is that conventional crystalline, constant-property models systematically overestimate safe operating windows, potentially leading to thermal damage. The developed framework provides a more accurate predictive capability for process design in solid-state thin-film battery manufacturing.

title: OptiMat Alloys: A FAIR End-to-End Agent with Living Database for Computational Multi-Principal Alloy Exploration
summary: The FAIR principles have transformed how computational data and workflows are shared in materials research, yet existing repositories can only serve pre-computed entries -- broad coverage is perpetually incomplete and cannot adapt to new questions on demand. To address these challenges, we present OptiMat Alloys, a large language model-powered conversational agent for multi-principal element alloy exploration built on three pillars: a living database that stores every calculation with provenance, low-barrier accessibility through a web interface requiring zero programming expertise, and built-in uncertainty quantification via cross-potential and cross-configuration validation (see demo here https://youtu.be/lQzuorkzPMc). Coupling foundational machine learning interatomic potentials covering near-all periodic table of elements with natural-language interaction, OptiMat Alloys enables targeted, on-demand computation guided by the user's domain knowledge-extending FAIR from pre-computed repositories to on-demand knowledge generation and making computational alloy screening accessible to any materials scientist.

AI.tldr: OptiMat Alloys is an AI-powered conversational agent for multi-principal element alloy exploration, transforming materials research from pre-computed repositories to on-demand knowledge generation.
AI.motivation: Existing FAIR-based repositories can only serve pre-computed entries with perpetually incomplete coverage and cannot adapt to new questions on demand. This limits researchers' ability to get targeted, timely answers.
AI.method: OptiMat Alloys is built on three pillars: (1) a living database storing every calculation with provenance, (2) low-barrier web interface requiring zero programming expertise, and (3) built-in uncertainty quantification via cross-potential and cross-configuration validation. It couples foundational ML interatomic potentials covering near-all periodic table elements with natural-language interaction.
AI.result: The system enables targeted, on-demand computation guided by user's domain knowledge, extending FAIR principles from pre-computed repositories to on-demand knowledge generation and making computational alloy screening accessible to any materials scientist.
AI.conclusion: OptiMat Alloys represents a significant advance in computational materials science by democratizing access to alloy exploration through natural language interaction, combining a comprehensive living database with uncertainty quantification to provide reliable, on-demand materials property predictions.

title: Extending Hamiltonian-Adaptive Resolution Simulation to Interfaces: An Updated LAMMPS Implementation and Application to Porous Solids
summary: Many natural phenomena involve processes that happen simultaneously at different characteristic length- and timescales. Typically, the region where the process of interest happens is affected by fluctuations in its surroundings. Modeling these systems requires an effective combination of simulation resolutions. The Hamiltonian-Adaptive Resolution Simulation (H-AdResS) method allows to model dual-resolution systems in length- and time-scales compatible with molecular diffusion, by combining atomistic and particle-based coarse graining models in the same simulation box. In this work, a new implementation of H-AdResS is provided in LAMMPS 2023. New features extend the usage to more diverse interaction potentials and simplify the preparation of input files via dedicated lammps input commands, while keeping the efficiency gain of the basis method. The implementation is benchmarked by reproducing water properties from a reference atomistic simulation. Importantly, the new implementation includes changes in compensation routines allowing to simulate systems with fluctuating density. As an example, the method in its new implementation is applied to modeling a porous metal-organic framework and its gas adsorption structure and transport properties. We demonstrate that structural and dynamic properties in the atomistic region of the dual-resolution scheme are unaffected and remain those of the fully atomistic system, while increasing simulation efficiency. This paves the way for using H-AdResS to simulate complex interfaces across applications in energy storage, electrocatalysis, and membrane technologies.

AI.tldr: A new implementation of Hamiltonian-Adaptive Resolution Simulation (H-AdResS) in LAMMPS 2023 enables dual-resolution molecular simulations combining atomistic and coarse-grained models, extending to diverse interaction potentials with simplified input preparation and applicability to fluctuating density systems.
AI.motivation: Many natural phenomena involve simultaneous processes at different length- and timescales, where the region of interest is affected by fluctuations in surroundings, requiring an effective combination of simulation resolutions to model these systems accurately.
AI.method: H-AdResS method combines atomistic and particle-based coarse-graining models in the same simulation box, with new implementation features including extended support for diverse interaction potentials, simplified input file preparation via dedicated LAMMPS commands, and modified compensation routines for fluctuating density systems.
AI.result: Successfully reproduced water properties from reference atomistic simulation; applied to modeling porous metal-organic framework and its gas adsorption structure/transport properties; demonstrated that structural and dynamic properties in the atomistic region remain unaffected while increasing simulation efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The new H-AdResS implementation enables efficient simulation of complex interfaces across applications in energy storage, electrocatalysis, and membrane technologies while maintaining accuracy in atomistic regions.
